SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000000.text: This net neutrality thing really affects people like me. My granddaughter takes engineering classes remotely from Sacramento and if companies started prioritizing websites based on who pays more, schools would suffer. Educational content shouldn't be subject to fast lane pricing. We need rules that keep internet access equitable.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000001.text: Without net neutrality, here's my fear: ISPs start charging agricultural software companies premium fees for full speed. Those costs get pushed onto us farmers. Corporate operations absorb it, but small operators like me—800 acres outside Decorah—can't compete. Net neutrality's the only thing keeping internet access fair for all farm sizes.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000002.text:
I am writing because my mother's story matters, and the internet is the only way it will survive after I'm gone. For three years now, I've been working to digitize her testimony—photographs, video, documents that span from her childhood in Warsaw through her liberation—and I cannot afford to have some internet provider decide that my nonprofit effort isn't profitable enough to move at normal speeds. My mother was silenced once, by history's worst machinery. I won't let her be silenced again by corporate throttling.

I spent forty years as an archivist. I know what happens when important records become inaccessible—they disappear, and we lose ourselves. Right now, there are thousands of small organizations like mine doing this work: preserving memory, documenting experience, making sure future generations understand what came before. We operate on donations and volunteer labor. We cannot negotiate with Comcast. We cannot pay premium rates for the bandwidth we need to serve people who want to learn.

The internet was supposed to be different from the old gatekeepers, but if we allow ISPs to decide who gets fast access and who doesn't, we're just building new gates. My archive won't generate advertising revenue. It won't make anyone rich. But it matters. History matters. The smaller voices, the ones that don't fit into some corporate algorithm—they matter most of all.

Please keep the internet open. Please.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000003.text: Been a piano teacher for roughly twenty years and what's happening with my internet is unfair. I stream recitals online for families including those out of the country. I sell backing tracks too. My service provider keeps slowing me down unless I pay extra fees. It's like paying twice for the same thing. Can't switch providers or set up multiple systems because of my back.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000004.text: The internet should be neutral, meaning no company gets to decide which websites run fast and which run slow based on who pays them. My granddaughter needs this for her engineering classes at UC Davis. If ISPs could throttle schools or demand extra fees, they're basically selling access to education itself.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000005.text: You know what's been bugging me? Our construction company needs to send job site videos constantly and Comcast keeps slowing everything down to a crawl. It's annoying and it costs us time and money but whatever. What really gets me is thinking about my kid. She's sixteen homeschooled and basically her whole education comes from YouTube tutorials and educational videos. Chemistry lessons, welding walkthroughs, documentaries, all on YouTube. If ISPs start charging extra to make certain sites load faster, what happens to families like mine? Suddenly my daughter's education depends on how much we can spend on internet.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000006.text: My entire livelihood depends on streaming competitive first-person shooters, and I'm genuinely worried about where this goes without net neutrality safeguards. My internet provider already throttled my gaming traffic—ping went from manageable to unplayable—because I wouldn't upgrade to their premium service tier. The frustrating part is I've had to spend over $12,000 out of pocket on equipment just to maintain competitive viability. That's money I shouldn't need to spend if ISPs treated all traffic equally.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000007.text: Piano instructor for twenty years struggling with internet throttling by my ISP. Stream recitals for families to watch online. Sell backing tracks for students. Getting charged extra for uploads feels wrong—like paying twice. Back issues prevent me from switching providers or setting up alternative systems. This situation is limiting my ability to earn.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000008.text:
I grew up in rural Montana without reliable internet, so I know what it's like when access becomes a privilege instead of a right. Now I'm at UCSB studying marine biology, and I'm running into a different problem—one that keeps me up at night actually.

My research focuses on kelp forest disease using satellite imagery. The datasets are massive. Like, we're talking terabytes of data that my university's internet struggles to handle even now. If ISPs start throttling academic and research traffic, smaller institutions and students like me get completely shut out. Only schools with enough money to pay for "priority lanes" would be able to do this work. That means marine conservation science becomes something only rich universities can afford to do properly.

I moved from a ranch town to California for school partly because I wanted better opportunities. But if net neutrality goes away, we're basically creating a new digital divide—not just between rural and urban areas, but between well-funded research programs and everyone else trying to do important work.

Look, I'm not gonna pretend I understand every technical detail here. But I understand what's at stake. We need the internet to stay neutral. Not just so I can download my datasets, but so that innovation and research doesn't become something only the wealthy can participate in. That's not how science should work.

Please keep net neutrality rules in place.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000009.text: Here's the reality: farming 800 acres without net neutrality protection puts me at risk. Technology requires reliable internet speeds. ISPs could throttle agricultural software unless companies pay premium rates, passing costs to farmers like me while corporate operations absorb expenses. Net neutrality keeps competition fair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000010.text: You need to understand what's at stake here. I manage 800 acres and use multiple tech platforms for irrigation, fertilizer timing, crop monitoring. These need consistent speeds. Without net neutrality, ISPs could prioritize whoever pays extra, which means corporate farms thrive while operators my size fall behind. That's not competition—that's rigged.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000011.text: Here's the reality: farming 800 acres without net neutrality protection puts me at risk. Technology requires reliable internet speeds. ISPs could throttle agricultural software unless companies pay premium rates, passing costs to farmers like me while corporate operations absorb expenses. Net neutrality keeps competition fair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000012.text: Been teaching kids piano for two decades now and honestly the internet situation is killing me. My students' families want to watch recitals online—their grandmother's in Mississippi, cousins overseas. I also sell practice tracks to students trying to learn. Simple enough right? Except my internet provider throttles my uploads unless I pay them extra money. Why should I pay twice to use the same internet everyone else has? I have back issues so I can't physically manage multiple setups. This is my livelihood and these companies are squeezing me.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000013.text: Here's the reality: farming 800 acres without net neutrality protection puts me at risk. Technology requires reliable internet speeds. ISPs could throttle agricultural software unless companies pay premium rates, passing costs to farmers like me while corporate operations absorb expenses. Net neutrality keeps competition fair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000014.text: As someone whose career depends on consistent internet performance for competitive FPS streaming, I can tell you net neutrality rules matter enormously. When my service provider throttled my traffic because I wouldn't subscribe to their premium gaming package, my ping jumped from 12ms to 45ms—absolutely unworkable for professional-level play. I've spent over twelve thousand dollars on equipment improvements to work around ISP discrimination. Removing these protections would devastate content creators like me.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000015.text:
I teach internet basics to seniors at the Burlington Public Library, and I need to be direct: net neutrality isn't some abstract policy question for my students. It's the difference between them being able to video call their grandkids and not.

Most of my learners are on Social Security. They come in twice a week to learn how to access Medicare portals, use telehealth appointments, manage their prescriptions online. These aren't luxuries—they're necessities that keep them independent. Right now, our library connection is equal: their video call loads at the same speed as anyone else's. They get dignity from that equality.

But if ISPs start creating paid fast lanes, if they throttle video content unless you pay premium rates, my students lose access. The library's budget is fixed. We can't absorb ISP surcharges so that Mrs. Patterson can see her doctor via video, or so Mr. Okafor can have a clear connection with his daughter in Lagos.

I also run our tech literacy grant program, which serves people making under 200% of the poverty line. We teach financial literacy, job searching, online safety. Without net neutrality, I'd have to tell these folks that the internet access they finally found affordable is only truly accessible if they pay more. That defeats everything we're trying to accomplish.

I've been a librarian for thirty years. We don't charge more for certain books. Internet shouldn't work that way either.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000016.text: As a small-scale agricultural entrepreneur in Alabama specializing in apiculture, I must express my strong opposition to any regulatory changes weakening net neutrality protections. My business model fundamentally depends on equitable access to internet resources. Video consultations with Auburn University agricultural specialists and online technical resources provide cost-effective alternatives to expensive in-person consulting. Permitting ISPs to implement tiered pricing or content prioritization would systematically disadvantage smaller producers relative to larger, wealthier competitors.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000017.text:
Look, I'm gonna be straight with you. I run a truck stop here in Breezewood and I've got maybe fifteen years left before I'm ready to hang it up. But right now what's happening with the internet is making it real hard to compete, and it's killing the small businesses around here.

My drivers need to use navigation apps, weather radar, freight-matching services—sometimes all at the same time. When the ISPs throttle certain traffic or charge premium rates, my WiFi costs go through the roof. I can't pass all that onto my customers because they'll just find another stop. Three other family-owned places closed in the last five years. Three. And it wasn't because they weren't good at what they did.

The big chains can negotiate deals with these providers. We can't. That's not competition, that's just getting squeezed out. 

I've been an amateur operator for a while now—call sign WB3KJL—and I do volunteer traffic reports on the CB for truckers. That works because nobody can charge me to transmit, nobody throttles my signal based on what I'm saying. That's the principle we need for the regular internet too. The roads should be neutral. The information highway should work the same way.

Don't let ISPs pick winners and losers based on what they can shake down for. Net neutrality isn't some radical thing. It's just fair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000018.text: This is Marcus Delaney from Alabama and I'm a beekeeper and I just want to make sure people understand how much internet matters to my business because it matters a lot. I've got forty thousand bees and when they get sick I need help and that help comes from videos online and from talking to smart people at Auburn on video calls and if internet companies start charging extra or making some websites faster than others I can't compete because bigger operations will have money to pay for that and I won't. It's unfair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000019.text: Honestly I'm not super political but this net neutrality thing affects me directly so here goes. Name's Marcus Delaney, I keep bees in Alabama, thousands of them, and basically my entire business runs on the internet. I need to watch videos about bee diseases, I need to video chat with experts, if that all starts costing extra money or gets slowed down, I can't keep up with bigger competitors who have the cash to pay for fast lanes. Doesn't seem fair to me. Small guys should be able to compete too.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000020.text: Professional competitive FPS streaming is my income source—not a hobby, actual bills and mortgage. My internet provider throttled my connection specifically because I wouldn't upgrade to their premium gaming package, tanking my ping from competitive 12ms to unplayable 45ms. I've invested over $12,000 in equipment just to partially mitigate their deliberate service degradation. Net neutrality protections are essential; without them, ISPs can extort creators through selective throttling.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000021.text: This is probably going to sound dramatic, but throttling my connection cost me real money. I stream competitive shooters professionally—that's my job, my mortgage, all of it. ISP intentionally slowed my gaming traffic to 45ms ping (was 12ms) after I wouldn't buy their premium tier. So I spent over twelve thousand dollars upgrading equipment to partially compensate. Without net neutrality enforcement, they can keep doing this, keep extracting money from creators who refuse their packages.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000022.text: So I run a competitive gaming stream for income—not a side gig, my actual paycheck. The ISP throttled me to 45ms ping after I wouldn't buy premium tier access, when I was sitting at 12ms before. For professional-level play that's devastating. Spent twelve grand on equipment upgrades because these companies won't guarantee equitable treatment. Without net neutrality rules preventing this behavior, creators like me are basically at their mercy for income extraction.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000023.text: Spent forty years playing music spent twenty of those teaching piano to young people. The internet situation I'm dealing with is genuinely making work harder. I stream recitals online so students' families can watch—grandmas in other states relatives overseas. I sell backing tracks online too. My ISP keeps slowing down my uploads unless I pay them extra money. That's not right that's paying twice for the same internet everyone uses. I have back problems that prevent me from setting up multiple systems or changing providers.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000024.text:
You know, I'm seventy-three years old, and I've spent my whole life working with language—translating Yiddish, which barely anyone studies anymore, writing down stories before they disappear. My mother was a survivor, and I have her testimony written out, along with interviews I've collected from maybe two dozen elderly speakers in my building, people who still dream in a language that's practically extinct. All of this lives on Google Drive because I'm on a fixed income and I can't afford a fancy backup system or the kind of broadband speeds that rich neighborhoods take for granted.

Here's what worries me sick: if the ISPs get to decide who gets fast access and who doesn't, people like me—and more importantly, the cultural heritage I'm trying to preserve—get left behind. I've already lost so much. I don't want to wake up one morning and find that my cloud storage is moving at a crawl, or that my files have been corrupted because the service degraded, or that I simply can't afford to keep uploading my work because the "premium" tier costs more than my medication.

The internet was supposed to be the great equalizer. For people without resources, without connections in publishing houses, without fancy studios—the internet was where you could build something, preserve something, share something that mattered. If you let the big telecom companies strangle that, you're not just slowing down data. You're erasing voices. You're making sure that only rich people get to leave a legacy.

Don't do this.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000025.text: You know, my granddaughter's been doing UC Davis classes from home and I keep thinking—if Comcast or whoever could slow down education websites or make them pay extra for decent speeds, that's basically a new kind of discrimination. We abolished old barriers to education; we shouldn't create tech-based ones.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000026.text:
I've been operating our church's livestream channel for going on four years now, and I need to speak up about what happens if the big internet providers get to pick and choose who gets good service and who doesn't.

We broadcast our Sunday services and Wednesday Bible study to shut-ins across the area—people in nursing homes, folks with mobility issues, some living alone out in the country where they can't get to church otherwise. Last year a woman in a care facility in Kinsley told me our stream was the only time she felt connected to her faith community. That meant something to me.

Here's what worries me: if Comcast or whoever decides that religious content costs extra to stream properly, or if they slow down YouTube so bad that our video keeps buffering, those homebound people lose that connection. A small church like ours can't pay premium fees to some internet company. We run on donations and volunteer time.

I work with vintage ham radios—been restoring them for decades—and something I learned from the old radio days is that when you control the pipes, you control what messages get through. The FCC understood that back then. These internet companies shouldn't get to decide which voices come through clear and which ones get strangled.

Keep net neutrality. Don't let them turn the internet into a toll road where only rich organizations get the fast lane.

Patricia Mewborn
Larned, Kansas

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000027.text: Look I'm a piano teacher been at it for twenty years and streaming recitals online so families can watch is part of what I do now. Grandparents in different states cousins overseas all want to see the performances. I sell backing tracks too it's honest income. What's not honest is my ISP slowing my uploads unless I pay them extra on top of what I already pay. It's double charging basically. My back won't let me set up multiple systems or switch providers so I'm stuck.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000028.text: This matters because my grandchild depends on internet access for remote university coursework. If broadband companies were allowed to slow down educational platforms or charge extra, they'd be creating artificial obstacles to learning. That's not okay. Net neutrality protects educational equity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000029.text:
Look, I'm going to be direct. Net neutrality isn't some abstract policy debate for me—it's the difference between life and death in an emergency.

Hurricane Maria. September 2017. Cell towers down across Puerto Rico for weeks. My family's neighborhood in Orlando had people helping coordinate rescue operations through internet-based forums and real-time satellite imagery. We needed that access *fast* and unthrottled. We were pulling engineering data, structural assessments, water distribution maps. An ISP deciding to throttle traffic to certain sites because they didn't pay a premium fee? That would've gotten people killed.

I work in aerospace—I design turbine components where margins for error are measured in microns. I understand systems thinking, redundancy, failure modes. Here's what concerns me: the internet has become critical infrastructure. Like it or not, it's as essential as power grids or water systems during disasters. I also sell practice tracks which is another income stream. My ISP keeps choking my upload speeds unless I pay them extra money. Seems wrong to charge me twice for the same internet access. Plus my health situation means I can't just switch providers or build multiple systems.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000072.text: Look, I keep bees. Been doing it for years here in Alabama and honestly without the internet I'd have quit a long time ago. I watch videos at like 2 AM trying to figure out what's wrong with a colony, I Zoom with people at the university—can't afford to fly there every time something goes wrong. But here's the thing: if these big companies start making you pay extra to watch videos or they slow down certain websites, I'm done. I can't compete with some big operation in Georgia that can just pay whatever fees to get faster access. It's not right.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000073.text: Beekeeping in Alabama, that's me. Got about forty thousand bees and honestly without internet access I'd probably have gone out of business years ago. I watch videos on YouTube—sometimes at ridiculous hours—trying to figure out what's wrong with my colonies. I talk to people at Auburn on Zoom who actually know what they're doing. If internet companies start charging premium rates for video streaming or play favorites with websites, I'm priced out. A bigger operation can afford whatever fees but I can't. That's not fair competition.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000074.text:
Look, I've been thinking about this a lot and I gotta say I'm genuinely torn here because there's legitimate concerns on both sides and I don't think anybody's being straight with us about what's really going on. My grandson is doing this online coding bootcamp right now—costs us a decent chunk of money every month—and he needs that connection to be reliable and fast, no throttling, no games. I get that. But then I also spend a lot of time researching how the big tech companies operate and honestly it worries me that they're basically running the whole internet now while the telecom companies get blamed for everything when maybe the real monopoly problem is with Google and Facebook and all of them deciding what information gets to move faster than other information.

I work part-time with Tesla's charging network so I understand infrastructure—you gotta maintain it, you gotta upgrade it, and that costs money. If you tell the ISPs they can't charge for different services then how do they pay for better pipes? But also I'm not stupid, I know they'll just raise everybody's bill and pocket the difference anyway.

What I want is for somebody to actually explain to regular people like me what net neutrality really means without the corporate BS. Will it help my grandson's bootcamp or hurt it? Will it actually stop these tech giants or just make them richer? Because right now I'm getting fed a bunch of garbage from both sides and I gotta make up my own mind but I don't have enough real information to do it properly.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000075.text: Been playing piano since I was young and teaching it professionally for about twenty years now. I stream student recitals online for families to watch including out of state and overseas relatives. Also sell backing tracks online which is part of my income. My internet service provider keeps slowing my uploads unless I pay more money. Feels like paying twice for one service. Because I have chronic back pain I can't physically manage multiple studio setups or easily switch providers unfortunately.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000076.text: This matters because my grandchild depends on internet access for remote university coursework. If broadband companies were allowed to slow down educational platforms or charge extra, they'd be creating artificial obstacles to learning. That's not okay. Net neutrality protects educational equity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000077.text: As someone whose career depends on consistent internet performance for competitive FPS streaming, I can tell you net neutrality rules matter enormously. When my service provider throttled my traffic because I wouldn't subscribe to their premium gaming package, my ping jumped from 12ms to 45ms—absolutely unworkable for professional-level play. I've spent over twelve thousand dollars on equipment improvements to work around ISP discrimination. Removing these protections would devastate content creators like me.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000078.text: This net neutrality thing really affects people like me. My granddaughter takes engineering classes remotely from Sacramento and if companies started prioritizing websites based on who pays more, schools would suffer. Educational content shouldn't be subject to fast lane pricing. We need rules that keep internet access equitable.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000079.text: Forty years playing music twenty years teaching it and my internet company is making it harder. Stream recitals online for students' families. Sell backing tracks. ISP throttles unless I pay extra. Feels like paying twice. Back pain limits my options. Need this throttling to stop.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000080.text: Precision agriculture is necessary now, not optional, for 800 acres. I depend on multiple software platforms requiring reliable bandwidth. Without net neutrality, ISPs could charge ag-tech companies extra fees, effectively creating slow lanes for farmers who won't or can't pay. That's unfair to independent operators.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000081.text:
I've spent thirty-five years helping people access information, and I've never seen anything quite like what's happened in my community over the past few years. We have seniors—many of them living on fixed incomes below the poverty line—who finally have reliable telehealth access through our library's broadband. Mrs. Okonkwo can check her blood sugar readings with her endocrinologist in Montreal without making a three-hour drive. Mr. Desrosiers manages his heart medication through his cardiologist's portal. These aren't luxuries. These are lifelines.

What terrifies me is the thought that an ISP could decide health sites are less profitable than streaming services and throttle them accordingly. Or worse, charge my students extra for "premium" access to the very sites their doctors need them to use.

I'm not a technologist—I'll be honest about that. I curate old computer manuals the way some people collect stamps. But I understand information systems, and I understand what happens when access becomes stratified. We've been down that road before in this country. Net neutrality is the only thing standing between the internet as a genuine public utility and the internet as a gated mall where ISPs decide who gets to see what.

Please keep the rules in place. The people I serve depend on it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000082.text: Eight hundred acres of crops require real-time data and consistent internet access. Soil modeling, weather analysis, equipment management—all impossible without reliable speeds. Net neutrality prevents ISPs from creating fast lanes that would price out or throttle independent farmers while favoring corporate agriculture.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000083.text:
Look, I'm just trying to finish my degree without going broke, and right now my ISP is basically making that impossible. I'm taking online classes through Northeast Mississippi Community College and trying to do my schoolwork on the WiFi at the gas station where I work because it's the only halfway decent internet I can access. Problem is, after I hit 50GB a month my speeds tank. Video lectures just buffer forever. It's like they're punishing me for actually using the connection I'm paying for.

If net neutrality goes away, I'm genuinely worried my provider is gonna start charging extra for "education tier" access or something. That's just another bill I can't afford on what I make pumping gas. Why should I have to pay extra just to watch a lecture for a class I already enrolled in? That doesn't seem right.

I know this probably sounds small compared to what big companies deal with, but to me it's everything. I'm trying to build something better for myself and this shouldn't be the thing that stops me. Keep net neutrality enforced. Don't let ISPs decide who gets fast speeds based on how much money they can squeeze out of people. We already don't have a lot of options out here in Mississippi anyway—most of us are stuck with whatever one company serves our area.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000084.text: Having devoted my career to understanding market asymmetries, I feel compelled to warn that the proposed net neutrality rollback reconstructs the conditions that nearly caused systemic collapse. Paid fast lanes fragment information distribution across internet infrastructure, creating advantages for those who can pay and disadvantages for everyone else. This mirrors the opacity that preceded 2008. It's not abstract either—my daughter runs a music streaming business and would be priced out of the market. We shouldn't engineer market failure into our infrastructure.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000085.text: Farming 800 acres of corn and soybeans requires precision tools that run continuously—soil sensors, weather feeds, equipment software. All need reliable internet speeds. Net neutrality matters because without it, ISPs could charge ag-tech companies premium rates for adequate service, costs that get passed down to farmers who can least afford it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000086.text: Twenty years teaching piano and streaming recitals online for families to watch. Also sell backing tracks. My internet provider throttles uploads unless I upgrade. That's paying twice for the same service. Back issues mean I can't physically switch providers or build multiple systems. This situation is genuinely harming my ability to work.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000087.text:
Look, I'm doing my online classes through Northeast Mississippi Community College and my internet at home is basically garbage. The gas station where I work has WiFi but it's not reliable enough for lectures, so I've been using it anyway because what else am I gonna do. My ISP caps video streaming after 50GB a month and then it just crawls. My instructor posts videos and suddenly I'm waiting five minutes for a ten-minute lecture to load.

Without net neutrality rules, they're gonna start charging extra for "education tier" access or whatever they want to call it. That's not fair. I'm already paying for internet. Why should I have to pay MORE because my school uses video? Community college is supposed to be affordable and right now it's barely working.

I also mess around with arcade machines as a hobby—I've got an old Pac-Man cabinet I'm restoring—and the speedrunning community I follow streams a lot of content. If ISPs can just slow down certain types of traffic or charge more for it, that kills what made the internet useful in the first place. Everyone should get the same speed. That's how it's supposed to work.

I know I'm just one person and I don't make much money, but I'm trying to improve my situation through school. Don't let internet companies make that harder by charging more for the stuff students actually need.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000088.text: Look, I don't usually comment on this stuff but I've been working with market data for like 20 years and something about allowing ISPs to charge for fast lanes just screams 'information asymmetry' to me. It's literally what happened before 2008 when some players knew things others didn't. Once you let that happen on the internet backbone, you're not just hurting small startups—you're creating systemic fragility. My kid runs a music platform and would be dead in the water. This matters more than we're treating it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000089.text:
I own a small donut shop in Long Beach and I've been in business for almost 26 years now. Started with $3,000 and a lot of determination. My story matters here because the internet is not a luxury for me anymore—it's how I survive.

Every single day I use YouTube to learn new recipes, watch business tutorials, check my suppliers' websites. My Instagram is how younger customers find us and see what's fresh. I compete against Krispy Kreme and Dunkin' by being nimble and real. Without that equal access, I lose.

But here's what really worries me. My two kids are taking college classes right now and they study at my shop because I can't afford a separate internet bill at home. My daughter's nursing school videos, my son's engineering homework—they need reliable speed just like anyone else. If ISPs start charging more for certain types of traffic, college students and small businesses like mine get squeezed out.

I've kept spreadsheets of every cost since 1998. I know margins. I know what breaks a business. A few extra dollars per month for "premium" internet access sounds small until you're running on 2-3% profit. That's money I'm not using for better ingredients or paying my workers fairly.

Net neutrality means the internet stays fair. Means my donut shop competes on quality and ideas, not on how much money I have to pay gatekeepers. That's all I'm asking for—a level playing field. That's the American way I believed in when I started this.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000090.text: I am Marcus Delaney, a beekeeper in Alabama, writing to express my concerns regarding potential net neutrality regulation changes. My operation depends entirely upon internet connectivity. I utilize video conferencing with Auburn University specialists and online educational resources to manage my forty thousand bee colonies effectively. Were internet service providers permitted to charge differential rates based on content type or to implement prioritization schemes, my business would face severe competitive disadvantages relative to larger operations with greater financial resources. This regulation is vital to small agricultural business viability.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000091.text:
The case for net neutrality isn't particularly complex once you work through the underlying economics, though I suspect the Commission already understands this and the debate is more about regulatory philosophy than actual analysis. I'm writing because I've watched what happens when gatekeepers gain pricing power in adjacent markets—the behavior is predictable, the competitive damage is measurable, and it compounds over time in ways that aren't obvious until you're already downstream from the decision.

My daughter runs a music production operation from our garage that, five years ago, would have been impossible without cloud infrastructure and reliable bandwidth. She competes with artists backed by major labels who have essentially unlimited infrastructure budgets. Under a non-neutral framework, those advantages become insurmountable—not because of superior creativity or business acumen, but because ISPs would have economic incentives to throttle her uploads or prioritize majors' content delivery. I've seen this pattern destroy competitive dynamics in finance repeatedly: the moment a player can pay for preferential treatment, innovation migrates upward toward incumbents.

There's also a secondary point about cultural preservation that seems underweighted in these discussions. I maintain a YouTube channel reviewing vintage synthesizers—12,000 subscribers, niche audience, virtually zero revenue. Under differential pricing schemes, that kind of hobbyist content creation becomes economically irrational to host or distribute. We'd lose the distributed creativity ecosystem that actually generates the ideas that eventually become commercially viable. Protecting net neutrality protects the conditions under which small-scale experimentation can flourish without needing venture capital or corporate permission structures.

This isn't radical. It's straightforward market structure analysis.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000092.text: So I'm a beekeeper here in Alabama and I have forty thousand bees and some of them are named after my daughter Sarah which probably tells you something about how much I care about this business. The internet is how I survive. Videos, university consultations, that's all critical. These internet companies want to charge more for video or treat websites differently and I'm just priced out. My bigger competitors will pay whatever fees are necessary and I won't be able to keep up. That's not how business should work.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000093.text: You know what's been bugging me? Our construction company needs to send job site videos constantly and Comcast keeps slowing everything down to a crawl. It's annoying and it costs us time and money but whatever. What really gets me is thinking about my kid. She's sixteen homeschooled and basically her whole education comes from YouTube tutorials and educational videos. Chemistry lessons, welding walkthroughs, documentaries, all on YouTube. If ISPs start charging extra to make certain sites load faster, what happens to families like mine? Suddenly my daughter's education depends on how much we can spend on internet.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000094.text: Teaching piano twenty years streaming recitals and selling backing tracks but my ISP throttles uploads unless I pay extra money. That's paying twice. Can't switch providers. Back problems. This is unfair and hurting my work.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000095.text:
I teach kids with autism, some of whom don't speak at all, and I'm watching them discover communication and learning through specific YouTube creators—people running channels from their basements on practically nothing. There's this one woman who does visual communication boards synced to indie music, another guy who breaks down phonics in ways that actually work for nonspeakers. Their content gets maybe a few thousand views. Under a paid prioritization system, these channels would be throttled into irrelevance while mainstream ed-tech companies with corporate budgets get the fast lane.

My students' families have spent *years* finding these resources. Years. And the creators spent years building trust and refining their approaches specifically for kids who learn differently. It's not like we can just switch to some algorithm-optimized corporate alternative—this stuff actually works because it's made by people who understand neurodiversity, not by whoever can pay for bandwidth.

I get that ISPs want to make money. I'm not naive about that. But net neutrality isn't about preventing profit—it's about preventing a two-tier system where access to vital educational tools depends on how much your creator can afford to pay. My students don't have a choice in what their schools provide them. We find what works *online*, and we're building their entire communication infrastructure around it.

If you kill net neutrality, you're not just slowing down some streaming service. You're actively blocking children from accessing the tools that help them communicate and learn.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000096.text: Been a piano teacher for roughly twenty years and what's happening with my internet is unfair. I stream recitals online for families including those out of the country. I sell backing tracks too. My service provider keeps slowing me down unless I pay extra fees. It's like paying twice for the same thing. Can't switch providers or set up multiple systems because of my back.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000097.text:
Look, I'm running a truck stop here in Carlisle and I gotta tell you straight—net neutrality matters more than people think, especially for folks like me who depend on the internet to keep things running.

My drivers need real-time GPS and weather data coming through fast. They need to communicate with dispatch without some company deciding "well, we don't like how you're using our pipes so we're gonna slow you down." That's basically what happens when net neutrality goes away. The big logistics companies? They can pay extra to get priority. What about me? I'm already running thin margins. I can't compete if Uber Freight or some massive corporation gets the fast lane while my drivers are stuck in the slow lane.

And it's not just business. I volunteer with the fire department too, and we use internet for emergency coordination. When seconds count, you can't have ISPs deciding which services matter more.

I taught myself networking and logistics software because I had to. I understand how this stuff works. Right now the internet treats all data equal, and that's the whole point. You start letting ISPs pick winners and losers, you're killing innovation and small business. Why would anyone risk starting something new if the big companies already paid for the better service?

Keep net neutrality. It's not complicated. It's fair, and it keeps people like me in business.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000098.text: This is probably important so I'm gonna write it down. My name is Marcus Delaney, I run bees in Alabama, about 40,000 of them (they're important to me, I even named some after my daughter Sarah). The internet keeps this whole thing going. Like literally. Without it I go out of business. I need videos, I need to talk to people at Auburn on Zoom, I need access to information and if big telecom companies start charging extra or slowing things down for certain content, I can't pay what bigger operations can pay so I lose and that's bad for competition and bad for people like me trying to make an honest living.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000099.text:
I own a truck stop here in Breezewood, Pennsylvania, been running it for twenty-three years now. My business is not just about the fuel—anybody can pump gas anywhere—it's about giving tired drivers a place where they can sit down, grab decent food, and most important, use WiFi that actually works so they can call their families, check on their kids, see their wives' faces on video before they sleep in the cab. That connection is everything to people who are away from home.

Right now my internet service is good enough that a driver stuck here overnight can stream a video call without it cutting out every thirty seconds. But if these big ISPs start charging extra fees for fast lanes or throttling down video traffic unless you pay more, that changes everything for my customers. A lot of these guys are independent contractors—they don't have corporate plans backing them up. If it costs them extra money just to see their family's face clearly, or if the connection gets deliberately slowed down so badly they can't use it, where does that leave them? Where does that leave my business?

I'm not anti-business or anything like that—I built this place from the ground up. But there's a difference between competing fair and squeezing people. Net neutrality means the ISPs have to treat all traffic the same, and that levels the playing field so a small operation like mine can compete, and ordinary people don't get nickeled and dimed just for basic internet that should already be available. My customers need this. I need this.

Please protect net neutrality.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000100.text: Piano teacher here with about twenty years experience under my belt. My students' families want to watch recitals on video so I stream them online. Extended family scattered around the country and world. I also make income selling backing tracks for music students. My internet provider keeps throttling my uploads unless I pay extra fees which honestly feels like paying twice for the same service. I have chronic back pain so physical options like multiple setups or provider switching aren't realistic for me.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000101.text:
I've spent fifty-two years as a librarian, and if I've learned anything, it's that information needs to flow freely or democracy itself starts to calcify. Now I'm retired and working on something that matters more to me than anything I did in those library stacks—I'm trying to get my mother's testimony about her experiences during the Holocaust onto the internet, properly archived, so it survives and reaches people who need to hear it. She's been gone for eight years now, and her words are all that's left.

Here's what terrifies me: if internet service providers can charge extra fees to make certain content load faster, what happens to independent archives like mine? What happens to all the stories that don't have funding behind them, that don't belong to some corporation with deep pockets? My mother's testimony isn't commercial. It won't make anyone rich. But it's the truth, and it deserves the same access as any Netflix stream or corporate website.

I still write my drafts on typewriters—I have twelve working machines, actually, collected them for years—and maybe that makes me old-fashioned, but it also makes me protective of information. Back in the library days, we didn't charge people more money to access certain books based on their income. The internet should work the same way. A teenager in a poor neighborhood deserves the same speed to find historical documents, to learn, to access culture, as someone whose family can afford whatever premium service the ISPs dream up next.

Please protect net neutrality. Please keep the internet a place where my mother's voice, and voices like hers, can reach whoever needs them most.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000102.text:
I've been running our church's livestream for almost eight years now, and I'm worried sick about what happens if net neutrality goes away. We broadcast our Sunday services to about forty people every week—sounds small, but most of them are shut-ins. Mrs. Henderson's got arthritis so bad she can't drive anymore. Mr. Paulson had a stroke last year. These folks depend on that stream like it's their lifeline.

My fear is simple: if the internet companies can start charging extra for certain kinds of content, or slowing things down, what happens to us? We're just a small rural church with a tight budget. We can barely afford the equipment we've got. I taught myself how to do the livestream by watching YouTube videos—no fancy setup. But if Comcast or whoever decides religious content needs to pay a premium, we're done. And these people lose their connection to their faith community.

I work with electronics as a hobby—I restore old ham radios, teach repair classes—and I understand how networks work on a basic level. The whole point of the internet was supposed to be open and fair. Everybody gets the same access. That's how it should stay.

Please don't let the big companies turn the internet into a toll road. Some of us depend on it for things way more important than streaming movies.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000103.text:
I'll be honest with you, I don't fully understand all the technical side of how the internet works, but I know enough to know we need net neutrality kept in place. Here in Logansport we're not close to anything—we're rural Indiana, about as rural as it gets. Our little Methodist church, we've got maybe forty-three people on a good Sunday, and a lot of them can't get out anymore. They're homebound. So we started doing these livestreams about three years ago and it's been a lifeline for some folks, literally. If the internet companies start throttling us or charging more because we're not some big outfit, we lose that connection to our congregation.

And then I substitute teach in the Logansport school system. Our district is already stretched thin on money. The teachers are doing remarkable work with what little we have. If ISPs can start charging extra for video content or slowing down educational platforms, we're the ones who get hurt worst. The kids in the cities with better funding, they'll be fine. We'll fall further behind.

I keep bees, that's another thing I do, and I mentor folks on local beekeeping. Most of what I share is through that Facebook group I run—videos, articles, advice. If those start getting slowed down or blocked, it affects a real community of people.

Net neutrality isn't some political thing to me. It's about fairness. Small places and small people need the same access as anybody else. That's just right.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000104.text:
I'll be honest, I'm not the type to usually weigh in on FCC stuff. I spend more time fixing up old radios than I do thinking about broadband policy. But my daughter's jewelry business got me paying attention to this net neutrality thing, and I can't stay quiet about it.

She's selling handmade pieces on Etsy, competing against companies with way more resources. If her shop has to load slower because some ISP decided to prioritize bigger sellers who can pay extra, that's not fair. That's not competition—that's just... gatekeeping. And I've thought a lot about what stewardship means in my faith, and it doesn't sit right with me that a few corporations get to decide who gets to compete on level ground.

Plus, I've been restoring vintage ham radios for years. You learn pretty quick that infrastructure matters. Whether it's radio waves or internet pipes, if access isn't there for everyone, you're not really building a free market. You're building a rigged one.

I know companies need to make money. I work in finance, I get it. But there's got to be a line between reasonable profit and just blocking people out because they can't afford the premium tier. The internet's become infrastructure now, like roads. And you don't charge some drivers more to use the highway than others just because they're small businesses.

Keep net neutrality. It's the right thing to do, and it actually lets innovation happen instead of crushing it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000105.text:
Look, I'm not going to pretend this is some abstract policy question. I work in systems—aerospace turbines, emergency preparedness—and I've seen firsthand what happens when infrastructure fails or gets weaponized by gatekeeping.

During Hurricane Maria, cell towers went down across Puerto Rico within hours. The only way my family and I coordinated our neighborhood's response was through internet access—unthrottled, equal access to engineering forums, real-time satellite imagery, NOAA data. We pulled people out of collapsed structures because we could share information freely and fast. If an ISP had decided to throttle access to disaster-relief sites or engineering resources because they weren't paying premium rates, people would have died. And I'm not being dramatic here.

I livestream motorcycle restoration work—vintage Honda rebuilds, therapeutic stuff I do in my garage—and my small audience relies on consistent, predictable speeds. But that's minor compared to the actual stakes. Without net neutrality, ISPs become gatekeepers deciding which emergency services, which medical information, which critical infrastructure data gets prioritized. That's not a market; that's rent extraction.

The argument that removing these rules will spur innovation doesn't hold up. Innovation thrives on open platforms. What we'll get instead is a tiered internet where money determines access, not merit or need. During the next disaster—and there will be one—we won't have the luxury of waiting for market forces to work themselves out.

Keep the rules. This shouldn't be complicated.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000106.text: Running a beekeeping outfit in Alabama for the past decade, I've learned that internet access isn't some luxury—it's infrastructure for survival. When my hives started showing signs of stress last spring, I relied on real-time video consultations with Auburn experts and late-night YouTube deep dives about colony collapse. If ISPs start charging premium rates for video content or throttling academic sites, small operators like me simply won't be able to afford staying current with best practices. Meanwhile, larger competitors with deeper pockets will pay for prioritized access to the same information. That fundamentally undermines fair competition.

Permitting ISPs to implement tiered pricing or content prioritization would systematically disadvantage smaller producers relative to larger, wealthier competitors.
