SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000114.text: Look, I've spent the better part of two decades in market analysis and what concerns me about this net neutrality rollback is how it mirrors the information asymmetries that caused 2008. When ISPs can create paid access tiers, you get winners and losers determined not by merit but by ability to pay. That's market failure dressed up as deregulation. My daughter runs a music streaming platform trying to compete in a fair market—this destroys that possibility. Beyond my family's stake, this is economically dangerous policy.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000115.text: This affects my livelihood directly. Farming 800 acres means relying on multiple software platforms for irrigation timing, fertilizer management, equipment operation. If ISPs are allowed to throttle services from companies that won't pay, my farm suffers. Net neutrality protects independent operators from getting priced out of modern agriculture.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000116.text: After forty years of musical work including twenty teaching piano I'm frustrated with my internet situation. Stream student recitals online for families. Relatives out of state and overseas. Sell backing tracks online. ISP throttles uploads unless I pay extra. That's double charging essentially. Back pain makes alternatives like multiple systems or provider switching impossible for me.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000117.text: The internet is literally my business's lifeline. I'm a beekeeper in Alabama—got 40,000 colonies that I've basically built my life around. When something goes wrong, I'm up at 2 in the morning watching YouTube videos about what might be killing them. I'm on Zoom with Auburn professors trying to figure out treatments. All of that stops working if companies start charging extra or creating slow lanes for certain content. I can't afford to pay more for the same information a bigger operation can access. It's a fairness issue.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000118.text: My family knows what educational barriers look like. My granddaughter shouldn't face digital ones. She needs reliable internet for UC Davis classes from home—and that means ISPs can't be allowed to slow education sites or charge premium rates. Net neutrality is essential for equal opportunity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000119.text: The way I see it, net neutrality protects opportunity. My Yuki sits at our kitchen table attending live lectures from UC Davis. If broadband companies could degrade school site speeds or charge universities extra for normal service, they're basically controlling who gets educated. My parents faced discrimination in education—I won't accept corporate gatekeeping instead.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000120.text: Can't let this slide without commenting. My family's history includes being shut out of educational opportunities. Now my granddaughter Yuki can learn engineering online from our kitchen. But that only works if ISPs can't throttle school websites or charge universities premium rates. Net neutrality keeps that door open.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000121.text:
I'm not going to pretend I understand all the technical stuff here, but I know enough to be worried. My oldest daughter runs a small jewelry business online—nothing fancy, just her designs on Etsy—and she's competing against sellers with way more resources. Right now at least the internet doesn't pick favorites. She gets the same bandwidth as anyone else. If that changes, if the big players can pay to move faster, she doesn't have a chance.

That bothers me more than I can probably explain without sounding preachy, but I grew up in a church that teaches we're supposed to be good stewards of what we're given. That's supposed to mean something. I work in municipal bonds, so I understand how money moves through systems, and I'm genuinely troubled by the idea of infrastructure working differently for different people based on who's got deeper pockets. It feels wrong.

I also restore old ham radios as a hobby—been doing it for years—and I spend a lot of time on the 40-meter band. All these restoration rigs, they work on the same basic principle: equal access, fair distribution. I know that's not how modern internet works exactly, but the principle matters to me.

The internet should stay open. Not because I'm some tech activist, but because I believe in people having a fair shot. My daughter deserves one. So does anyone else trying to build something.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000122.text: Maybe I'm just complaining but this internet throttling is real and it's affecting my ability to work. I've been a piano teacher for about twenty years and I stream my students' performances online so family members can watch even if they're far away. I also sell practice tracks which is another income stream. My ISP keeps choking my upload speeds unless I pay them extra money. Seems wrong to charge me twice for the same internet access. Plus my health situation means I can't just switch providers or build multiple systems.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000123.text:
I'm seventy-three years old and I've spent most of my life working with language—translating Yiddish texts, trying to preserve something that's disappearing faster than anyone wants to admit—and I'm writing to you because I'm genuinely frightened about what happens to people like me if net neutrality disappears.

My mother survived the war and spent the last thirty years of her life telling me stories in Yiddish, and I've been recording elderly speakers in my building here in Brooklyn, neighbors mostly, people in their eighties and nineties who still remember the old country. I've got nearly a thousand video files I'm trying to organize and back up before I die, and frankly, before they do. That work lives on a shared Google Drive because I can't afford the kind of internet speeds you get if you're paying premium rates, and I certainly can't afford to pay more if some ISP decides my files are lower priority than streaming video for someone richer.

My unpublished memoir—my mother's survival story that I'm still working on, that I've been working on for fifteen years—is up there too. Everything on cloud backup because I learned the hard way that computers fail. But if the ISPs start throttling based on what they decide is valuable traffic, what happens to archivists like me? To people on fixed incomes trying to preserve their families' histories? To the Yiddish speakers whose voices will be gone by the time anyone decides they matter?

You can't let them do this. Some of us don't have resources to compete in a tiered internet. Some of us are just trying to keep something alive.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000124.text: This regulation directly impacts my farm's viability. I manage 800 acres using technology requiring consistent bandwidth—soil analysis, weather services, equipment software. Net neutrality prevents ISPs from throttling these essential services, protecting independent operators from broadband discrimination.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000125.text:
The internet has become essential infrastructure in ways that economists like myself are only beginning to adequately model, and the current proposal to permit prioritization schemes fundamentally misunderstands the nature of that infrastructure. I'm not going to invoke some abstract principle here—I'll be concrete: my daughter applied to a competitive STEM summer program last year, and her application was rejected not because she lacked qualifications, but because her school in rural Michigan couldn't reliably upload the required video submission. Her public school's bandwidth constraints, which persist because the private market has found rural broadband insufficiently profitable, created an artificial barrier to entry that had nothing to do with merit.

This is an efficiency problem, not merely a fairness problem. When I analyze capital markets, I look at transaction costs and information asymmetries—these distort pricing and prevent optimal allocation. Net neutrality, properly understood, is about preventing artificial costs that don't reflect underlying scarcity. If ISPs can charge content providers for priority access, we introduce a rent-extraction layer that doesn't improve network capacity; it simply redistributes who captures the value of that capacity. That's economically pernicious.

I spent this weekend restoring an old Yaesu FT-101 ham radio—these devices work because of open standards and non-discriminatory access to spectrum. The internet succeeded for similar reasons. The moment we permit gatekeeping based on willingness to pay rather than technical merit or actual congestion, we've created a system fundamentally misaligned with innovation incentives. That's not regulation; that's preventing regulatory capture by incumbent ISPs.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000126.text:
Look, I'm in commercial real estate here in Houston, so I deal with small business owners every single day. Most of my clients are contractors—good people, mostly Black-owned firms trying to build something real in this city. And you know what they all need? Reliable internet to submit bids, manage their crews, track inventory. That's not optional anymore.

If you let ISPs start charging premium rates for faster access or throttling bandwidth based on who can afford what, you're basically rigging the game before these guys even get a chance to compete. A small contractor can't afford to pay Comcast or whoever extra just to load their project management software at the same speed as some big national firm. That's not competition—that's a toll road where the guy with less money gets stuck in the slow lane.

I've spent enough time in nature—actually, I'm out birding most Sunday mornings around the coast—to know how ecosystems work. You need a level playing field or everything gets out of balance. Internet access is the same way now. These small businesses are part of Houston's backbone. If ISPs can carve them out and charge them extra, or slow them down, we all lose.

Keep net neutrality in place. Don't let them create a two-tier internet where money decides who gets speed and who doesn't.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000127.text: Perhaps I'm overthinking this but net neutrality seems really important for educational access? My granddaughter attends UC Davis remotely from Sacramento and relies on consistent internet speeds. If companies could slow down school platforms or charge them extra fees, wouldn't that reduce educational opportunities for students whose families can't afford premium connections?

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000128.text: My family knows what educational barriers look like. My granddaughter shouldn't face digital ones. She needs reliable internet for UC Davis classes from home—and that means ISPs can't be allowed to slow education sites or charge premium rates. Net neutrality is essential for equal opportunity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000129.text:
Look I'm not gonna pretend I understand all the technical stuff about how the internet works but I know what's happening to me right now and it sucks. I'm taking classes online through my community college and my ISP already slows down my video lectures during certain times of day. Like I'll be trying to watch a calc video at 6pm and it buffers every thirty seconds. If companies can start charging extra for "fast lanes" then the college is gonna have to pay more money just to let me actually access my own classes. That's backwards.

I work at a Speedway making minimum wage. I can barely afford tuition as it is even with community college being cheaper. My parents can't help me out. The internet is literally the only reason I can go to school at all instead of just pumping gas forever. If ISPs get to decide who gets fast speeds and charge for it then people like me are just locked out. How is that fair?

This isn't about wanting free stuff. I'm willing to pay for internet service. I'm asking for the same speeds I'm already paying for to not get worse because some company decides my college isn't important enough to pay their special fee. 

Keep net neutrality. Please. Some of us actually need it to have a shot at anything better.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000130.text: I'll admit upfront I don't really understand the technical mechanics of ISP operations but I understand the impact on my life perfectly well. My construction business depends on uploading video documentation constantly and Comcast has throttled service to the point where jobs take significantly longer. Beyond the business impact though I'm concerned about my homeschooled sixteen year old daughter who relies entirely on YouTube based educational content. If ISPs implement paid prioritization for certain sites that fundamentally threatens her access to quality education and our family's ability to provide it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000131.text: Just want to be clear on something: I run a bee operation here in Alabama and the internet isn't extra for me, it's necessary. When my hives get sick I'm watching videos and talking to university people on Zoom. That knowledge is what saves my business. Let internet companies start charging different rates for video or slowing down certain websites and I can't compete anymore. Bigger guys with bigger budgets will get faster access to the same information I need. Seems wrong to me.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000132.text: Name's Marcus Delaney. I keep bees in Alabama. Not sure how much else I need to explain but here goes anyway. My business lives and dies by internet access. I need to watch videos about bee diseases at weird hours, I need to video call with people who went to college and actually studied this stuff, and if internet companies start charging more for some kinds of data and less for others, I'm done. My bigger competitors will just pay whatever it costs. I won't be able to afford to keep up. Seems fundamentally unfair to small operators.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000133.text:
I'm a fly fishing guide up here in Livingston and I've built my entire business around Instagram. My clients find me through video reels—sunset on the Yellowstone, a cutthroat hitting a dry fly, stuff like that. Those videos are how I book my trips, and I'm making decent money doing what I love. Eighty grand a year isn't rich but it lets me stay here instead of moving to some city.

Here's the thing: if my internet provider can slow down my uploads or make Instagram load faster than other platforms just because they own some competing service, I'm done. I can't compete. A guide in Wyoming whose ISP favors their own streaming service will get priority, and I'll get throttled. My content won't upload fast enough to stay visible. People will book someone else.

I'm not a tech guy—I barely finished college and most of what I know about the internet I learned by accident—but I know unfair when I see it. I've spent thirty-four years keeping detailed notes on insect hatches. I'm digitizing all of it now because I want that knowledge preserved. If the internet becomes a rigged game where money determines what people can actually see, then all of that work, all of that data, becomes worthless.

Net neutrality isn't some abstract policy thing. It's the difference between me staying in business or having to leave Montana to find work somewhere else. Keep the internet fair. That's all I'm asking.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000134.text: Here's my situation: I teach piano. I've been doing this professionally for about twenty years. My students' families want to watch recital videos online—grandparents in other states, family overseas. I also make money selling backing tracks to people learning music. My internet provider keeps slowing down my uploads unless I pay them more money. This doesn't feel right. It's like having to pay twice to use the same road. I have health issues that limit my physical options so I can't easily switch providers or set up alternative systems.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000135.text: This is Marcus Delaney from Alabama and I'm writing because this net neutrality thing actually matters to me in a real way. I've got bees—forty thousand of them—and keeping them alive requires internet access that costs the same as it costs everyone else. I watch videos, I talk to people at Auburn, and that all matters. If big internet companies start charging extra or creating fast lanes for certain content, smaller operations like mine just lose. Bigger beekeepers with more money will get better internet access than me. That's not how competition should work.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000136.text: Honestly I'm not super political but this net neutrality thing affects me directly so here goes. Name's Marcus Delaney, I keep bees in Alabama, thousands of them, and basically my entire business runs on the internet. I need to watch videos about bee diseases, I need to video chat with experts, if that all starts costing extra money or gets slowed down, I can't keep up with bigger competitors who have the cash to pay for fast lanes. Doesn't seem fair to me. Small guys should be able to compete too.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000137.text: Running a beekeeping outfit in Alabama for the past decade, I've learned that internet access isn't some luxury—it's infrastructure for survival. When my hives started showing signs of stress last spring, I relied on real-time video consultations with Auburn experts and late-night YouTube deep dives about colony collapse. If ISPs start charging premium rates for video content or throttling academic sites, small operators like me simply won't be able to afford staying current with best practices. Meanwhile, larger competitors with deeper pockets will pay for prioritized access to the same information. That fundamentally undermines fair competition.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000138.text: The technical details of internet service provision admittedly escape me, however I feel compelled to address a pressing concern affecting both my livelihood and my family. As a small business owner in construction, video uploads have become essential to our operations. Comcast's service throttling has substantially degraded performance. More concerning, however, is the potential impact on my homeschooled daughter's access to educational content. Should broadband providers begin implementing differential pricing schemes, educational equity for lower-income families would be severely compromised.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000139.text:
Look, I've been a union plumber for six years now, and I'm also the steward for Local 12, which means I spend a lot of time thinking about how we train the next generation. Right now we're building out remote apprenticeship modules—streaming video lessons, interactive troubleshooting sessions, the whole thing—because not every kid in Dorchester has access to a good trade program nearby. It's working. We've got forty apprentices enrolled.

If ISPs start throttling union bandwidth or charging extra for "priority" streaming, that program dies. Simple as that. These are kids whose families can't afford private training, and we're talking about kids in neighborhoods where broadband access is already spotty.

I also do contract work maintaining internet infrastructure in public housing complexes. I see firsthand how people in those buildings depend on consistent, affordable connectivity. When the service gets degraded because some ISP wants to squeeze more money out of essential services, it's not just inconvenient—it's discriminatory.

People say net neutrality is complicated. It's not. I've read every major FCC decision back to the Communications Act of 1934. We've done this before. We decided telephone lines had to be common carriers because access was essential. Same logic applies now. The internet is essential infrastructure. You don't let utilities pick winners and losers based on who pays them the most.

Keep the neutrality rules in place. Don't let ISPs become gatekeepers to opportunity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000140.text:
I need to be direct here. I pastor a Methodist congregation in Martinsville, and we're small—34 members, mostly elderly—but we're holding on because of the internet we have. Our shut-ins watch Sunday service streams. Two of our members do telehealth appointments because there's no specialist within an hour's drive. One woman in her eighties gets her medication dosage reviewed over video because her daughter is a nurse in Indianapolis and helps coordinate her care. That's real life out here.

What worries me is this: if Comcast or whoever controls the lines decides that religious organizations don't merit priority bandwidth, or that a nonprofit can't afford the fast lane, then we lose those people. We lose the income. We lose the community.

I'll admit something else. I've been teaching neighborhood kids—teenagers who'd otherwise leave Martinsville because there's nothing for them—how to build and maintain a community mesh network. We've strung lines across three county blocks using salvaged equipment. It works because we're not competing against prioritized traffic. The moment ISPs can throttle traffic based on who's paying what, that whole experiment collapses.

Net neutrality isn't some abstract principle to me. It's the difference between a rural Methodist church surviving or closing its doors. It's whether a kid in Indiana can learn something practical and stay connected to his town. Without it, we're not competing on an even field anymore—we're just waiting for the corporations to decide our worth.

Keep the internet neutral. Please.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000141.text:
I'm a veterinarian working with cattle ranchers across North Dakota, and I need the FCC to understand that net neutrality isn't some abstract internet debate for me—it's a matter of animal welfare and whether rural practices can survive.

When I'm called out to a ranch forty miles from the nearest town, I use telemedicine to consult with specialists in Minneapolis in real time. I upload ultrasound images and bloodwork straight from my truck. If my ISP decides to throttle medical data so they can prioritize their streaming video package, cattle die. That's not hyperbole. A retained placenta or a difficult calving doesn't wait for buffering.

My ISP already bundles in entertainment services I don't use. If they start creating fast lanes for their own content while slowing down the bandwidth I actually need for diagnostics, I'm stuck. I can't switch providers out here—there's basically one option. I have ham radio equipment in my shop from the 1970s that still works better than my current internet some days. Back then, communication infrastructure was treated as essential. It still is.

Throttling medical applications to prioritize entertainment destroys the premise that rural areas can participate equally in modern practice. A vet in Minneapolis won't have these problems. Neither will ranchers in California. Just us.

Keep net neutrality. Don't let ISPs decide what data matters more than other data.

Marcus Thiel, DVM
Bowman, ND

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000142.text:
Look, I'm not some tech guy—I still don't fully understand how half this stuff works. But I know what happens when my upload speeds tank, and that's real money walking out the door.

I built my guide business on showing people what's actually happening on the water right now. My clients scroll through my Instagram, see the river conditions in real time, see the fish rising, and they book a trip. That's 47,000 followers who depend on those videos being there when they check their phones. If Comcast or whoever decides to slow down my uploads because I'm not paying them extra, while some big tourism company with deep pockets gets priority, then I'm done. Simple as that.

I also run a fly-tying channel on TikTok—tie custom flies, sell them online. My 5 AM streams reach people all over the country. That's a real business. Rural Montana doesn't have the bandwidth of Bozeman or Billings. We're already working with half the speed. The last thing we need is ISPs creating a fast lane we can't afford.

This isn't about wanting free stuff. I pay my bill every month. It's about keeping the playing field level so a small business owner in Livingston can compete with big outfits in cities that have better infrastructure to begin with. Kill net neutrality and you're basically telling rural businesses we don't matter.

Keep the internet open.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000143.text: Sir or madam: I am writing regarding net neutrality regulations as they pertain to my agricultural business in Alabama. I maintain approximately forty thousand honeybee colonies. Internet connectivity is essential to my operational procedures—I rely upon video consultation with Auburn University specialists and online resources for colony management. Should internet service providers be authorized to charge differential rates or implement prioritization schemes based on content type, my competitive position would be substantially undermined. Regulatory protection of net neutrality is economically critical.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000144.text:
I've spent twenty-three years in public libraries—first in Ann Arbor, now here in Burlington—and what I'm watching happen with net neutrality concerns me deeply, not as some abstract principle but because I see the real harm in my senior tech literacy classes every single week.

These are people on fixed incomes. Social Security, maybe a small pension if they're lucky. Last spring, one of my students—Eleanor, seventy-three—needed to attend a telehealth appointment for her cardiologist. Her regular internet speed was already marginal. The clinic's video conferencing system kept dropping her connection. When I explained that her ISP could theoretically throttle her access unless she paid for a "premium" tier, she just went quiet. That silence told me everything.

The thing is, this isn't obsolete—to borrow a word from my other passion, competitive Scrabble—this is current and urgent. We're talking about people trying to access job retraining programs, virtual doctor visits, online banking for people who can't physically get to branches anymore. These aren't luxuries. They're infrastructure now.

What kills me is that we already have the technology. The barriers are purely financial and regulatory. If we allow ISPs to create fast lanes for those who can pay, we're literally pricing people like my students out of essential services. That's not a market failure we should accept. That's a policy choice.

Kill the tiered internet. Keep net neutrality. Your most vulnerable citizens are counting on it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000145.text:
Look, I organize tenants for a living. Low-income folks in Oakland—people barely hanging on. We use digital tools to coordinate, to share information fast, to build power. Last year my internet got throttled during a tenant meeting on Zoom. Video was freezing up right when we needed to show evidence of code violations. That's not coincidence.

I also run a community aquaponics project with neighborhoods dealing with food insecurity. We share growing data, troubleshoot systems online, teach people how to grow their own food in small spaces. That only works if everyone's got equal access to decent bandwidth. I'm not paying Comcast extra so my neighbors in West Oakland can upload their farming videos at normal speed.

I get that I'm not a policy expert. Didn't finish law school for a reason. But I understand what happens when you let companies pick winners and losers on the internet. The people I work with don't have leverage to pay for "premium" access. They get squeezed out. Their voices literally don't travel as far.

By the way—I do competitive cup stacking and I've got a YouTube channel with decent engagement. I know what it looks like when platforms can control who sees what. That's exactly what happens when net neutrality dies.

Keep the open internet open. Don't let ISPs decide which content matters more. My communities depend on it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000146.text: Look I'm a piano teacher been at it for twenty years and streaming recitals online so families can watch is part of what I do now. Grandparents in different states cousins overseas all want to see the performances. I sell backing tracks too it's honest income. What's not honest is my ISP slowing my uploads unless I pay them extra on top of what I already pay. It's double charging basically. My back won't let me set up multiple systems or switch providers so I'm stuck.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000147.text: I'm just a grandparent trying to understand policy but it seems clear—if ISPs can charge more for certain websites or slow them down, educational access becomes a commodity only rich families can afford. My granddaughter Yuki shouldn't face corporate gatekeeping just to attend her UC Davis lectures online.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000148.text: My 800-acre farm depends on internet-connected technology—can't avoid it. Precision agriculture software, real-time weather data, soil analysis tools all need consistent speeds. Net neutrality prevents ISPs from throttling these services based on payment arrangements, which would unfairly advantage corporate farms over independent operators.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000149.text:
Look, I'm going to be straight with you—I run tenant organizing in Oakland, which means I'm coordinating with people who can barely afford their internet bill as it is. We use video calls, shared documents, all of it to help folks understand their rights. Last year I watched Comcast throttle our connection during a housing justice meeting. The video kept freezing, people were dropping off. Tell me how that's not interference.

But this isn't just about tenant work. I also help run a community aquaponics network—we grow food in neighborhoods where people don't have reliable access to fresh produce, and we share data and tutorials online so more people can do this in their own space. That only works if the internet stays open. If ISPs start deciding which websites load fast and which ones don't, smaller operations like ours get crushed. We can't pay for "fast lane" access.

I get that companies want to make money. I'm not naive about that. But net neutrality isn't about killing innovation or whatever—it's about keeping the playing field level enough that someone like me, or a nonprofit, or a community project, can actually reach people without getting squeezed out by whoever has the deepest pockets.

My YouTube channel about speed-stacking has 12K subscribers. Small audience, but real people who care about the content. That only exists because the internet isn't a toll road. Keep it that way.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000150.text:
Look, I'm a marine bio student at UCSB and I work part-time at an aquaculture facility. Most of my research depends on accessing open databases and open-access journals that honestly aren't hosted on fancy servers with corporate backing. If ISPs start throttling traffic based on what they think will make them money, that's going to kill opportunities for students like me who don't have access to expensive institutional subscriptions.

Here's the thing—reproducible science only works if everyone can access the same data and methods. I've been looking at kelp forest ecosystem models that were published by researchers at smaller labs, community colleges, that kind of thing. Those resources aren't going to pay Comcast or whoever to get priority bandwidth. So either the internet stays neutral and I can do my research, or it doesn't and my generation gets locked out of science that should be public knowledge anyway.

I get that this is probably boring compared to streaming and whatever, but this actually matters. And yeah, I realize I sound like I'm being dramatic, but I've literally written about how predator-prey dynamics work in ecosystems and how that relates to pinball machine flippers (it's weird, I know). The point is systems only work when everything has equal access to information. Break that and the whole thing falls apart.

Please keep net neutrality. We need it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000151.text: My granddaughter does her UC Davis engineering coursework from our house here in Sacramento using our internet. If AT&T or Comcast starts degrading speeds for school sites unless professors pay premium rates, that's wrong. My parents couldn't choose their own education—they faced systemic barriers. Yuki shouldn't face corporate barriers instead.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000152.text: Farming 800 acres of corn and soybeans requires precision tools that run continuously—soil sensors, weather feeds, equipment software. All need reliable internet speeds. Net neutrality matters because without it, ISPs could charge ag-tech companies premium rates for adequate service, costs that get passed down to farmers who can least afford it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000153.text: To whom it may concern, I operate a small agricultural enterprise in Alabama specializing in honeybee management. Internet access is absolutely essential to my operational capacity. Video consultation with Auburn University specialists and online technical resources enable me to manage colony health effectively and prevent disease. Any regulatory changes permitting content-based pricing or ISP-level prioritization would place smaller enterprises at a severe disadvantage relative to larger competitors. I strongly advocate for continued net neutrality protection.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000154.text:
I grew up in rural Montana without internet until I was fourteen, so I know what it's like when infrastructure doesn't reach you. Now I'm at UCSB doing marine biology research, and honestly, net neutrality isn't some abstract policy thing to me—it's whether my lab can actually do the work we need to do.

I'm studying kelp forest disease using satellite imagery datasets. These files are huge. Like, gigabytes huge. My university's internet can handle it, but barely, and that's only because UCSB has resources. A lot of schools don't. If ISPs start charging extra for "research-grade" speeds or prioritizing certain traffic, you're basically saying that marine conservation science—actually, all science—becomes something only wealthy institutions can afford to do properly. That's broken.

The thing that gets me is that this affects everything downstream. If smaller schools and research centers can't access the data fast enough, they can't contribute to solving problems. Climate change, ocean acidification, disease tracking in ecosystems—these are time-sensitive things. We can't wait for data to load at throttled speeds.

I don't think ISPs should be able to decide which research matters based on what generates them the most money. The internet worked because it was basically fair. Keep it that way. Don't let them turn it into a toll road where only the richest labs can afford the fast lane.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000155.text:
Look, I'm going to be straight up—my lab's research depends on this. We've got sensors in the kelp forests off campus that stream data in real-time to partner universities in Chile and Japan. If Comcast or whoever decides to throttle our packets because we're not paying their special internet tax, our NSF grant literally gets torpedoed. We're talking about $80K worth of research into ocean acidification that just... stops working because the data can't move fast enough.

And yeah, I get that companies want to make money. But net neutrality isn't about killing their business model—it's about keeping the internet actually useful. Fifteen years of my mother's survival stories, handwritten by me, typed up, edited, sitting in the cloud because my internet in Brooklyn is already slow enough that I can barely afford it on my fixed income. I'm not asking for charity. I'm asking for the internet to stay the way it's supposed to be—fair. If the big companies can decide to slow down my files because I can't pay extra, or because my memoir doesn't make them money, or because they just feel like it, then what happens to all of us who are trying to save things, to document, to leave something behind that matters?

I've translated from Yiddish my whole career. I know about loss. I know what happens when you don't preserve what you have. Please don't let them break the internet. Not for people like me. Not for anyone.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000192.text:
Look, I work with my hands for a living and I've spent the last three years helping set up streaming video infrastructure for our apprenticeship program in Dorchester. We're teaching kids real skills—plumbing, electrical, HVAC—through remote instruction because not every family can afford to pull their kid out of work for years. The videos are on Local 12's bandwidth, paid for by our union health fund.

Here's what keeps me up at night: if Comcast or whoever decides union training content isn't profitable enough to prioritize, they can slow it down. They can make it buffer. They can make it worthless. And suddenly some kid in Upham-Hastings can't access the curriculum because his ISP decided our apprenticeship program wasn't worth full speed.

I also spend weekends helping fix internet lines in the public housing complexes around here. I've seen what happens when service gets degraded—it's always the neighborhoods that have the least money to fight back. Net neutrality isn't some abstract policy thing. It's whether people with less money get locked out of opportunity.

I've read the FCC's decisions going back to the Kingsbury Commitment. We've been regulating utilities like this for nearly ninety years because when you don't, companies optimize for profit, not people. The internet's a utility now. Treat it like one.

Keep net neutrality. Don't let them sell fast lanes.

This is wrong structurally AND morally.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000202.text:
I'm seventy-three years old and I've spent my entire career preserving other people's words—organizing archives, cataloging documents, making sure that fragile pieces of history didn't disappear. Now I'm trying to finish something that matters more to me than anything I did professionally: my mother's testimony about her years in the camps. She wrote it out by hand in the 1970s, and I've been scanning photographs, converting her voice recordings, gathering video from relatives who want to contribute their own memories before it's too late.

Without net neutrality, I'm terrified. I don't have money to pay some internet company extra fees just to upload these files at a decent speed. These aren't commercial projects—they're historical documents that need to survive. My archive is going to a nonprofit that serves educators and students. We're not generating profit. We're doing the work that matters most.

I train therapy dogs—I've got one named Miriam right now, after my grandmother—and I take her to nursing homes where people are the last living witnesses to entire worlds that vanished. If the internet becomes a two-tiered system where only wealthy institutions can afford to move their materials quickly, where do the rest of us go? Where does a retired woman in Brooklyn preserve her mother's voice?

The internet should not be a luxury service. History should not be a luxury. Please protect net neutrality.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000203.text:
Look, I'm going to be honest—I'm not the most tech-savvy person out there, and half the time my grandkids have to help me figure out my email. But I've been teaching science for thirty-five years and running a farm for twenty, and I understand systems. I understand what happens when you have unequal access to resources.

My CSA business operates entirely online. Customers order their vegetables through my website, and if an ISP could decide that some big corporate agriculture distributor gets faster speeds than my little operation up here in Hardwick, that's not free market competition—that's rigging the game. I can't compete with that. I built something real here, something my community depends on, and it works because the internet is neutral ground.

But here's what really gets me: I mentor students remotely through video calls—science instruction for kids in rural areas who don't have access to the resources we had in the city. One of my former students is now at UVM studying environmental biology because we could work together on projects online without buffering, without her connection getting throttled while some streaming service got priority. If that changes, rural education suffers. That's not theoretical—that's my students' futures.

And frankly, I run an online forum for heritage poultry genetics with members from six countries. It's not profitable. It's a resource for small farmers trying to preserve genetic diversity. An ISP could quietly slow us down, make us invisible, and nobody would even notice. But these communities matter.

Please don't let them do this.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000204.text:
I need to speak plainly about what net neutrality means for us here in Martinsville, because if you strip away the regulations, you're not just affecting tech companies—you're affecting churches like mine.

We have thirty-four members, and about half of them are homebound or elderly. Every Sunday, we stream our service. Not as some luxury—as a lifeline. Mrs. Chen broke her hip two years ago and hasn't been able to physically attend, but she's still part of our community through that stream. When our internet gets throttled during peak evening hours, she misses worship. That matters spiritually, but it also matters financially. That's tithe money we need to keep the lights on, to run our food pantry.

Then there's telehealth. We don't have specialists in three counties. Our congregation uses online doctors for dialysis scheduling, diabetes management, mental health counseling. If Comcast decides to slow down medical video calls unless we pay extra, what happens to people who can't afford it?

I'll be honest—I've also spent the last five years teaching kids to run a community mesh network with salvaged equipment across three county blocks. I'm not doing anything illegal, and I'm keeping kids engaged who might otherwise leave. But that project only works because we've had open internet to learn from. If the playing field gets tilted toward corporate ISPs, that dies too.

Net neutrality isn't about ideology for us. It's about whether rural Americans stay connected to each other and to the services we need to survive.

I keep spreadsheets of every supplier cost since 1998. I know what it takes to survive. Internet access is like electricity now—it's basic infrastructure. Letting ISPs pick winners and losers means small shops like mine lose, and families like mine suffer.

Thank you,
Sophea Ung
Long Beach, California

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000223.text: Here's my situation: I make my living streaming competitive shooters. Seriously. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000224.text:
I need to be direct about something I've watched happen to rural America, and it's not theoretical for us—it's happening right now in Martinsville.

Our Methodist congregation numbers 34 people. We're not declining because faith is fading; we're shrinking because young people have to leave to find work. The ones who stay, the elderly, the disabled—they depend on streaming our Sunday services when they can't make it to the building. One of our members, Mrs. Koehler, hasn't missed a service in forty years, but her arthritis means she watches from home now. When the internet crawls during peak evening hours, she gets buffering instead of communion. That matters spiritually and financially—we need those tithe dollars to keep the lights on.

I've spent three years building a community mesh network using salvaged equipment, teaching teenagers the skills they need to stay competitive. It's kept some of them in town. But here's what keeps me up: if Comcast decides to throttle nonprofit traffic, or if they prioritize corporate streaming over our little operation, we don't have the resources to fight back. We'd simply disappear.

Net neutrality isn't about ideology for us. It's about whether a rural Methodist church, a homeschool co-op, a volunteer fire department—whether any of us deserve the same internet access as Indianapolis. We do. And if you remove the rules protecting that, you're effectively choosing which communities survive.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000225.text:
I'm seventy-three years old and I've spent most of my life preserving other people's stories in libraries and archives, which means I understand in my bones how fragile memory is, how easily the important things disappear if we don't fight to keep them visible and accessible. Right now I'm working on something that matters more to me than anything I've done professionally—my mother's testimony about her experiences during the Holocaust, along with photographs and video interviews with survivors who are, frankly, not getting any younger. I need to upload these materials, high-resolution images and hours of video, and I need the internet to work the same way for me as it works for anyone else, not slower because some corporation decides my nonprofit historical project isn't profitable enough to prioritize.

The notion that internet providers should be able to throttle or block content based on who's paying them the most is obscene to me. It means that stories like my mother's—stories that should be preserved and studied and remembered—could be pushed into the slow lane while some streaming service loads instantly. It means smaller archives, community projects, nonprofit organizations get squeezed out by whoever has the deepest pockets. I've trained therapy dogs for nursing homes for fifteen years, named every one after relatives I lost, and I see how technology can connect people across distances, how it can matter. But only if everyone has fair access to it.

Please protect net neutrality. History depends on it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000230.text:
I spend my days teaching seventy-year-olds how to video chat with their grandchildren and access their Medicare portals. They come to the library because they can't afford high-speed internet at home. They're on fixed incomes. Most of them were skeptical about technology until they realized it could connect them to doctors without leaving Burlington in February.

If ISPs are allowed to create paid fast lanes, those telehealth appointments—the ones that keep my patrons out of the emergency room—become inaccessible. A Netflix stream might buffer. A Medicare portal takes five minutes to load instead of one. This isn't abstract for me. This is Mrs. Kowalski asking why her blood pressure readings suddenly won't upload, and me not having an answer except "the internet company decided that feature costs extra now."

The internet was supposed to level things. That's what drew me to technology education in the first place. I came from music theory into librarianship because I believed in democratizing access to information. Net neutrality is the basic infrastructure for that belief to actually work.

I understand ISPs invest in their networks. That's real. But once you allow tiering of content based on who pays, you've decided that access to telehealth, to education, to job applications—all of it—is a luxury good. You've decided my patrons don't deserve the same speed as people in cities who can afford $150 a month for "premium" internet.

Please protect net neutrality.
