I'm writing this as someone who's spent forty years teaching kids to think critically about systems—and right now, the system we're discussing is broken in ways that matter deeply to rural communities like mine.

My farm depends on precision agriculture software. I monitor soil conditions, milk production, and equipment remotely. The company I use competes with others that my ISP might prefer to prioritize. If Comcast decides to throttle their data while speeding up a favored competitor's, I lose access to the tools that make my operation viable. That's not competition. That's extortion dressed up as business.

But here's what really worries me: my grandchildren. I grew up isolated in rural Vermont—the nearest decent library was twenty miles away. The internet changed that for kids out here. They can access college courses, learn from experts worldwide, apply to scholarships. But only if ISPs can't turn the internet into a toll road where the poor can afford the slow lane and everyone else pays premium prices to reach educational content.

Net neutrality isn't some abstract principle. It's about whether a retired teacher and part-time farmer has fair access to the same tools as industrial operations. It's about whether a kid in Craftsbury can compete with a kid in Boston. It's about whether innovation can come from anywhere, not just from whoever can pay an ISP for priority access.

Restore net neutrality. It's that simple.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000232.text: My operation's 800 acres of corn and soybeans won't survive without reliable internet access to ag-tech platforms. If net neutrality disappears and ISPs start charging premium rates for speed, smaller farms get squeezed out. I can't compete with mega-operations that can afford throttled connections—this rule keeps the playing field level.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000233.text:
I run a fly fishing guide business out of Livingston and I've built it almost entirely through Instagram. My clients book trips because they see my videos—sunrise on the Yellowstone, a cutthroat coming to a dry fly, that kind of thing. I post several times a week and those videos have to upload fast or they get buried. That's not some luxury, that's literally how I make my living. Eighty grand a year doing what I love.

If internet providers start throttling video or giving preferential treatment to big media companies that can pay for it, I'm finished. A small business like mine can't compete with Netflix or whatever if the pipes aren't neutral. My connection isn't great out here anyway—I'm already at the mercy of one provider. The last thing I need is them deciding my content doesn't matter because I'm not paying extra.

I've kept detailed journals on insect hatches for thirty-four years. Thirty-four years. I'm digitizing all that now because I want to share what I've learned, help other anglers understand the water better. That takes bandwidth. If net neutrality goes away, that knowledge stays in notebooks instead of helping people.

This shouldn't be complicated. A level playing field means I get to compete on the quality of my work, not on who's got the deepest pockets. Keep the internet neutral. Don't let ISPs pick winners and losers.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000234.text: Eight hundred acres near Decorah won't be profitable without reliable internet access to farming technology. Net neutrality ensures my ISP can't create a two-tiered system where wealthy corporations get fast access and smaller operations get throttled. This rule keeps agriculture fair across all farm sizes.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000235.text:
Look, I'm going to be straight with you. I've been running my truck stop here in Breezewood for twenty-three years, and right now the internet is killing me—not because there's too much of it, but because the big providers are choking it off.

My drivers need to pull up navigation, check weather, match freight loads. They're doing this all at the same time. Three, four apps running. Used to work fine. Now my ISP's got me on this "premium tier" because they say my WiFi is heavy usage. Heavy usage. It's literally their product I'm trying to sell. Meanwhile, they're not throttling the guys at the big travel centers because those corporate folks can afford to pay whatever. That's not right.

I've watched three local truck stops close down in the last five years. Good people. They couldn't compete because they couldn't afford what the ISPs were charging. I'm probably next if this doesn't get fixed. And the thing is, I know networks. I've been restoring old radio equipment for forty years, running my own CB broadcasts for truckers on weather and road conditions. I get how bandwidth works. This isn't about the internet being congested—it's about companies deciding who gets to use it fast and who doesn't.

That's not what the internet is supposed to be. You need to protect net neutrality. Let the companies compete on actual service, not on who can pay the biggest shakedown fee.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000236.text:
I work at the Burlington Public Library, where I teach digital literacy to seniors—most of them on Social Security, living month to month. Net neutrality isn't abstract policy to me. It's my student Margaret trying to access a telemedicine appointment for her rheumatoid arthritis without paying extra for "medical-grade" internet speeds. It's Carl, who's retraining for a different career at 71, needing reliable access to online coursework that shouldn't cost him fifty dollars more a month just because it involves video streaming.

Last month, a grandmother came in worried because her grandchild's school said Zoom classes would require upgraded service. A *public school*. The family couldn't afford it on her fixed income.

We've already seen what happens without these protections. ISPs are testing our patience—and our wallets—with these tiered schemes. The people I teach aren't the ones who can simply pay more. They're the ones who fall off the digital map entirely when costs spike.

I've been a librarian for thirty-four years. Access to information is my life's work. The internet isn't a luxury good anymore—it's infrastructure, like roads or water. When you let companies create toll lanes, you're creating a two-tiered society where only the affluent get genuine access.

Keep net neutrality rules in place. Seniors, students, people with disabilities, low-income families—we all depend on equal access. That's not radical. That's just basic fairness.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000237.text:
Look, I've been thinking about this net neutrality thing for a while now and honestly I go back and forth on it. My grandson's doing this coding bootcamp online, right, and he needs good internet that doesn't lag or he can't do his assignments right, and I see how important that is. But then I also see these big tech companies pushing around the smaller internet providers, and I'm not sure the FCC understands what's really going on there either.

I work on Tesla Superchargers part-time now that I'm retired from the union, and I see how one company can basically control things when they get big enough. That worries me. But I also don't want government getting too heavy-handed because government screws things up just as much as anybody else does, maybe more. I've been watching some videos about how the 5G infrastructure is being rolled out and who's really benefiting, and it's not always the little guy you'd think.

What I know is that my grandson should be able to use his internet without some ISP choking it down because he's using a service they don't like. That just seems wrong to me. Same time, I don't want AT&T or Comcast getting strong-armed by Google or whoever into giving away service for free to big companies while regular people like me pay full price.

Maybe there's a middle ground here where we just make sure things stay fair without letting the monopolies run the show, whether that's the tech companies or the internet companies. That's all I'm saying.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000238.text:
Look, I've been in the trades for eleven years now, and I've seen what happens when infrastructure gets carved up by people who don't care about the people actually using it. Right now I'm helping put together an apprenticeship program for kids in Dorchester—union apprentices learning plumbing, electrical, HVAC through streaming video curriculum. That's how we're training the next generation. If Comcast or whoever decides they can throttle union bandwidth because we're not paying their special fees, that program dies. Those kids don't get trained. Simple as that.

I also spend weekends helping get decent internet into public housing complexes. I'm not a tech guy—I can barely work my own laptop—but I know what I see out there. When ISPs can pick winners and losers on the internet, they pick themselves. They pick whoever pays. They don't pick working people trying to skill up or families trying to stay connected.

I've actually read the old utility cases going back to like 1934. The FCC figured out a long time ago that when one company controls the pipes, you need rules to make sure they don't squeeze out everyone else. That's not politics. That's infrastructure policy that's kept this country working for nearly a century.

Net neutrality isn't perfect, but killing it so AT&T can charge extra for bandwidth? That's not progress. That's just rent extraction. Don't do it.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000239.text:
Look, I'm a chemistry teacher in Shaker Heights and I need to be straight with you about what's happening in my classroom. We don't have the budget for a lot of things anymore—they cut my prep periods three years ago, which means I'm basically doing my own lesson planning on nights and weekends. But here's what I *do* have access to: YouTube, Khan Academy, some free simulation sites. That's it. That's how my students are learning the concepts they need.

Last semester, I noticed something. During the school day, those videos would buffer for like thirty seconds between every couple minutes of content. My students would get frustrated and give up. I started having them watch tutorials at home instead, but then I realized—not all of them have decent internet at home. The ones in nicer neighborhoods? Their videos load instantly. The ones in older parts of town? They're stuck waiting around.

I started researching this and found out it might be throttling from the ISP. That's when it hit me that this isn't just annoying—it's actually creating inequality in my classroom. A student's access to educational resources shouldn't depend on whether their ISP decides to slow down the sites they're using.

I also run a pickling business on the side to fund classroom equipment since the school won't. I sell through farmers markets and I've had to use data heavily—I couldn't do that if websites were being choked out. But more importantly, my students deserve the same internet speed to learn as kids in wealthy districts. That's the real issue here.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000240.text: My operation's 800 acres of corn and soybeans won't survive without reliable internet access to ag-tech platforms. If net neutrality disappears and ISPs start charging premium rates for speed, smaller farms get squeezed out. I can't compete with mega-operations that can afford throttled connections—this rule keeps the playing field level.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000241.text:
I'm a fishing guide here in Livingston and I run my business almost entirely on Instagram. Every client who books a trip with me found me through video reels—sunset catches, rising trout, that kind of thing. It's how I make my living, and it works because I can upload content fast enough to stay visible in the algorithm.

If ISPs start throttling video or if they can pay to speed up certain content over others, I'm done. My internet already isn't great out here—I drive to town sometimes just to upload a decent reel. But right now at least it's equal. My videos move at the same speed as anybody else's. If some big corporation can pay to jump the line, a small operator like me gets buried.

This isn't about being anti-business. I get it, ISPs want to make money. But net neutrality keeps the playing field level for people like me. I'm 61 years old. I didn't go to tech school. I know trout and I know how to tell a story with a camera. That's my skill. I shouldn't have to negotiate with some telecom company to reach my customers.

I've been keeping detailed journals about this river for 34 years. I'm digitizing them now because the data matters and I want to share it. But if the internet becomes tiered, that project and my whole income stream both go away. Keep net neutrality. Let people compete based on what they're actually good at, not on who can afford the fastest lane.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000242.text: This is Marcus Delaney from Alabama and I'm a beekeeper and I just want to make sure people understand how much internet matters to my business because it matters a lot. I've got forty thousand bees and when they get sick I need help and that help comes from videos online and from talking to smart people at Auburn on video calls and if internet companies start charging extra or making some websites faster than others I can't compete because bigger operations will have money to pay for that and I won't. It's unfair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000243.text: To the regulatory authority: I am a beekeeper in Alabama operating a small-scale enterprise dependent upon internet connectivity. Video conferencing with Auburn University specialists and online technical resources are integral to my colony management protocols. Net neutrality protections ensure equitable access to these essential business resources. Permitting internet service providers to implement content-based pricing or prioritization would create significant competitive disadvantages for smaller operations relative to larger enterprises. Such regulatory changes would be harmful to small agricultural business viability and fair market competition.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000244.text:
I'm seventy-three years old and I've spent my entire career—forty-two years as a librarian in Brooklyn—fighting to keep information accessible to anyone who walked through our doors, regardless of their circumstances. I'm writing because I'm watching the internet become something antithetical to everything I believed libraries should be.

Right now I'm working on something that matters more to me than anything I've done professionally: getting my mother's testimony about her experiences during the Holocaust onto the internet where it can be preserved and found by anyone searching for it. She passed in 1996, but her voice—recorded on cassette tapes I've been transcribing by hand onto my typewriters, yes, I still use them, twelve of them actually—needs to survive. Needs to be accessible.

If internet service providers can decide which content moves fast and which moves slow based on who's paying them, what happens to the independent digital archives? What happens to the small museums and the grassroots historians and the people like me trying to preserve stories that the commercial interests have no reason to amplify? The Holocaust testimony that nobody's going to make millions streaming—that gets throttled, forgotten, lost. Again.

The internet was supposed to be different from the old gatekeepers. It was supposed to be the great equalizer. Net neutrality isn't some abstract principle—it's the only thing standing between my mother's voice and oblivion. It's the only thing that says her story matters as much as whatever Netflix or Disney decides to push into fast lanes.

Don't let them carve it up.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000245.text:
Look, I've been thinking about this net neutrality thing for a while now and I gotta say I'm all twisted up about it. On one hand, my grandson's doing this coding bootcamp online—real intensive stuff, needs solid internet connection, low lag time, the whole deal—and if the ISPs start throttling that kind of traffic to push their own services, that kid's education gets hurt. I've seen enough in my union days to know when somebody's trying to squeeze working people, and that's what this feels like.

But then I'm watching all these videos about how Big Tech companies like Google and Amazon are basically running the internet anyway, deciding what we see, what we don't see, and maybe the phone companies need some leverage to keep them honest. I don't know. Could be I'm wrong about that part.

What I do know is I fix WiFi problems for half my block, and people depend on that. Internet's not fancy anymore—it's like electricity was in my grandfather's time. You can't just let one company decide who gets it fast and who gets it slow based on what makes them the most money. That's not how utilities should work.

My concern is that however this gets decided, somebody's gonna get screwed, and it'll probably be regular folks like my grandson trying to better themselves. We need the Commission to actually think about the little guy for once instead of just listening to lawyers and lobbyists who can afford fancy dinners.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000246.text: The concern is real and immediate for me. Operating 800 acres requires constant access to precision agriculture systems—soil monitoring, weather analysis, equipment software. Net neutrality means my internet provider can't discriminate based on which ag-tech company I'm using or how much they've paid. Without it, service becomes inconsistent and unfair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000247.text:
I work at my church in Larned running our YouTube livestreams on Sunday mornings. We're not talking about some big operation—it's just me and a couple volunteers with equipment we fundraised for over three years. But what we do matters. We've got maybe 200 people watching every week, and half of them can't leave their homes. Some are in nursing facilities two counties over. Some have mobility issues. Some live alone and this service is honestly their main connection to the church community.

Last year I thought about what happens if our internet provider decides religious content costs extra to stream smoothly, or if they slow it down because it doesn't make them money. I actually called them and asked and they wouldn't give me a straight answer. That scared me.

I've been fixing old ham radios as a hobby for years—I teach a class on it—and you know what? Back then, the airwaves belonged to everybody. You could build something and reach people without some company deciding you didn't pay enough. That's what net neutrality means to me. It means the internet stays open like those old radios were.

If you kill net neutrality, my church's channel might become too slow to watch reliably. Those elderly folks will lose their lifeline. And it won't be because we did anything wrong or because there's actually a technical problem. It'll be because somebody decided our content wasn't worth their bandwidth.

Please keep net neutrality in place. These people deserve better.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000248.text: I need to explain my situation because it affects me directly and I think it's important people understand. I'm a beekeeper in Alabama with about forty thousand bees and some of them are named after my daughter Sarah which tells you how much this matters to me. The internet isn't a luxury for my operation—it's survival. I'm watching YouTube videos at two in the morning trying to save sick colonies, I'm on Zoom calls with Auburn experts, and if internet companies start charging different amounts for different types of content, I'm out. I can't compete with bigger operations that can pay whatever fees.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000249.text:
Look, I've been running my truck stop here in Carlisle for twenty-three years. I know what it takes to survive in this business, and right now I'm worried sick about what happens if the FCC doesn't protect net neutrality.

My operation depends on things most people don't think about. My drivers are out on the road using GPS, pulling weather data, checking their dispatch apps—all real-time stuff. We use logistics software that tracks fuel prices, route optimization, you name it. If some big ISP decides they can throttle that traffic because it doesn't benefit them, or they slow it down unless we pay extra fees, that's it for guys like me. I can't compete with the big trucking companies that can afford to pay whatever premium charges these ISPs want to charge.

And it's not just about money. Safety depends on this too. My guys need clear, fast communication if there's a breakdown or an accident. You slow down that data flow and now we're talking about real consequences.

I've memorized trucking regulations for forty-eight states—I take compliance seriously. But I can't regulate the internet companies. That's the FCC's job. We need rules that say everybody gets equal access, period. Small operators can't survive if the playing field gets tilted this way.

Don't let them tell you this is about innovation. It's about control. Keep net neutrality in place.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000250.text:
I spend a lot of time thinking about puzzles—how pieces fit together, how one small rotation can unlock possibilities. Net neutrality isn't abstract to me the way it might be for someone else.

Three times a week I teach basic internet literacy at the Burlington Public Library. My students are mostly older adults on Social Security, maybe some younger people with disabilities or recent immigrants. They're learning to access their Medicare accounts, video chat with grandchildren out of state, find health information their doctors recommend. These aren't luxury activities. They're essential.

Here's what keeps me up: if we lose net neutrality, the ISPs decide who gets fast lanes and who doesn't. That means the telehealth appointments I teach my students to schedule could buffer and timeout because they didn't pay extra. The Medicare portal becomes unusable. The YouTube video from their cardiologist's office about their new medication loads at a crawl, if at all.

I understand ISPs invested in infrastructure. But the internet isn't like cable TV anymore—it's basic infrastructure, like roads. You don't charge different prices depending on which store someone's driving to.

The library has limited bandwidth to begin with. We can't absorb throttling costs. My students definitely can't. Most are living on $15,000 a year. If ISPs start creating paid fast lanes for specific services, we're essentially pricing people out of healthcare and civic participation.

Please protect net neutrality. My students deserve the same internet experience as anyone else, regardless of their income.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000251.text: Teaching piano for twenty years, streaming recitals for families, selling backing tracks online. My ISP throttles uploads unless I pay extra—that's double charging. Can't switch providers because of chronic back pain. This is harming my livelihood and needs to change.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000252.text: To whom it may concern: As someone who actively manages honeybee colonies in Alabama, I write to advocate for maintaining net neutrality protections. Internet connectivity functions as essential infrastructure for my agricultural operation. Consultation with university specialists via video conferencing and access to technical resources online represent critical components of effective colony management. Permitting service providers to implement paid prioritization schemes would create unjust market conditions favoring larger, better-capitalized competitors. This regulatory change would disproportionately harm small-scale agricultural enterprises.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000253.text:
I've spent forty years teaching kids about ecosystems and interconnectedness, so maybe that's why net neutrality feels so obviously important to me—it's just another system where everything depends on fair access.

Here's my practical concern: I run precision agriculture software on my farm. GPS-guided irrigation, soil monitoring, predictive analytics for herd health. It all streams data back and forth constantly. If Comcast decides that some competing farm tech company's data gets throttled while their preferred vendor moves at full speed, I'm stuck. I can't afford to switch entire systems. That's not competition—that's extortion dressed up as a business model.

But honestly, the bigger picture keeps me up at night. I grew up rural in the seventies. The isolation was suffocating. My grandkids have a real shot at futures beyond farming or retail because the internet opened up the world to them. They take online classes, they're learning coding, they're getting mentored by people three states away. Net neutrality is what makes that possible. The moment we let ISPs pick winners and losers based on who can pay for fast lanes, we're right back to geography being destiny.

I'm not naive about how the internet works or what it costs to maintain. I understand the infrastructure argument. But "costs money to build" isn't the same as "therefore some companies should get preferential treatment." We maintain rural roads without charging FedEx different rates than local delivery trucks.

Keep the internet open. It's not radical. It's just fair.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000254.text: I am Marcus Delaney, a beekeeper in Alabama, writing to express my concerns regarding potential net neutrality regulation changes. My operation depends entirely upon internet connectivity. I utilize video conferencing with Auburn University specialists and online educational resources to manage my forty thousand bee colonies effectively. Were internet service providers permitted to charge differential rates based on content type or to implement prioritization schemes, my business would face severe competitive disadvantages relative to larger operations with greater financial resources. This regulation is vital to small agricultural business viability.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000255.text:
Look, I'm going to be real with you—I organize tenants in Oakland, which means I spend a lot of time coordinating with people across different neighborhoods, people who don't have money for fancy internet plans, and if ISPs start deciding which websites load fast and which ones don't, we're basically handing them the power to silence organizing efforts they don't like. I've already noticed our Zoom calls getting choppy right when we're trying to document slumlord violations, and I can't prove it's intentional but it feels intentional, you know?

The thing is, the internet right now is one of the few tools we have that's actually democratic, where a group of renters in West Oakland can communicate just as easily as a group in Piedmont, and that matters for power. Poor communities can't compete with landlords' lawyers and PR teams unless we can organize fast and cheap across fragmented neighborhoods, and we do that through whatever bandwidth we've got. Once you let Comcast and these companies start charging more for certain traffic, they're not going to charge more for real estate developer websites—they're going to make it harder for us.

Also I don't think people realize how much low-income communities depend on the open internet for everything else too. I consult on urban mushroom cultivation, help people grow food in their apartments, and that information needs to flow freely. When you have net neutrality, knowledge isn't gatekept by who can afford the premium lane.

Just keep it open. That's all we're asking.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000256.text:
Look, I'm not going to pretend I know all the technical stuff about how the internet works, but I know what's happening to us and it's not right. I run a construction crew across New Mexico and Arizona, and we're using Comcast for our job site coordination—uploading photos, sending plans, streaming safety training videos to our guys. That's how work gets done now. But Comcast keeps throttling our uploads unless we pay their premium business tier, which costs almost as much as our internet for the whole office used to cost. That's not competition, that's just them squeezing us because they can.

Here's the thing—I also organize the competitive eating circuit on Discord, which sounds random I know, but we've got people flying in from all over for events, and we coordinate everything through that platform. Fast, reliable internet at a fair price isn't some luxury. It's how small organizations like ours actually function. Whether it's my union trying to train people or my eating community organizing tournaments, we all need the same basic access.

Without net neutrality rules, companies like Comcast just pick winners and losers. They're not going to help anyone but themselves. I've seen this play out in construction—the big guys get the best deals, the rest of us get squeezed. That's not how it should work on the internet either. You've got to keep the rules in place. Don't let them break it again.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000257.text: As someone whose career depends on consistent internet performance for competitive FPS streaming, I can tell you net neutrality rules matter enormously. When my service provider throttled my traffic because I wouldn't subscribe to their premium gaming package, my ping jumped from 12ms to 45ms—absolutely unworkable for professional-level play. I've spent over twelve thousand dollars on equipment improvements to work around ISP discrimination. Removing these protections would devastate content creators like me.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000258.text: Look, I keep bees. Been doing it for years here in Alabama and honestly without the internet I'd have quit a long time ago. I watch videos at like 2 AM trying to figure out what's wrong with a colony, I Zoom with people at the university—can't afford to fly there every time something goes wrong. But here's the thing: if these big companies start making you pay extra to watch videos or they slow down certain websites, I'm done. I can't compete with some big operation in Georgia that can just pay whatever fees to get faster access. It's not right.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000259.text: So I'm not the most tech-savvy person around admittedly, but I can tell you firsthand that something's wrong with how my ISP operates. Running a small construction outfit requires constant video documentation—site conditions, contractor feedback, approval workflows. Comcast throttles these transmissions routinely. The monetary costs are real. Yet frankly the broader issue concerns me more: my homeschooled sixteen-year-old depends entirely on internet-based educational resources. A tiered ISP pricing model would effectively create educational barriers for middle-class families.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000260.text:
I'm a pastor out here in Logansport, and I've got to speak up about this net neutrality thing because it's affecting my congregation in ways I don't think most people realize. We're a small Methodist church—only 43 members—and a good portion of them are elderly or homebound. On Sunday mornings we livestream our service so these folks don't get left behind. It costs us nothing to stream on equal footing with everybody else right now. But if the big internet companies can start charging extra for that traffic, or slowing it down because we can't pay their fees, then what? We don't have a budget for that. Period.

And I substitute teach at the school district here, so I see firsthand how thin the margins are. The kids need to watch educational videos, especially since rural schools like ours can't always have specialists come in person. If ISPs start throttling that content or charging premium rates, the district has to choose between fixing the roof and paying for decent internet speeds. That's not a real choice.

Look, I'll admit I don't understand all the technical side of this stuff. I'm still figuring out why my church Facebook page looks the way it does. But I understand fairness, and I understand that when you've got limited resources—whether you're a small church or a small school district—you get squeezed when big corporations control the rules. Net neutrality is the only thing keeping the playing field even for people like us.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000261.text:
So I'm sitting here at like 3 in the morning again (nursing session, but also I was actually debugging some Rust code I've been playing with) and I keep thinking about how my husband won't be able to see his daughter's face clearly on video calls if ISPs start throttling video platforms. He's deployed right now with the Coast Guard, gone for months at a time, and honestly? Those calls are what keep us sane. Our daughter's six months old and he's missing so much already.

The thing is, we don't have a ton of options out here in Yachats for internet providers. It's basically one or two, and if they decide that FaceTime or Zoom or whatever video platform doesn't fit their business model, they can just make it unusable. Like, technically it'll work, but it'll buffer constantly, drop, lag so bad you can't really see each other. That's not actually "working."

I run the goat dairy too and we're starting to use more video for farm management stuff—checking on things remotely when I can't be there, coordinating with local suppliers. And my transcription work obviously depends on reliable upload speeds for files. But mostly I just keep coming back to the fact that my husband paid the same monthly bill I did and he should get the same quality service, especially for something as basic as video.

I've contributed to some open-source projects (I maintain a whole color-coded spreadsheet tracking them, yes I'm that person) and a lot of that work depends on net neutrality principles. If internet is treated as a service where some data matters more than other data based on who can pay, innovation gets weird and gatekept.

Just please don't let them throttle this away.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000262.text: To whom it may concern, I operate a small agricultural enterprise in Alabama specializing in honeybee management. Internet access is absolutely essential to my operational capacity. Video consultation with Auburn University specialists and online technical resources enable me to manage colony health effectively and prevent disease. Any regulatory changes permitting content-based pricing or ISP-level prioritization would place smaller enterprises at a severe disadvantage relative to larger competitors. I strongly advocate for continued net neutrality protection.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000263.text: Dear Sir or Madam, I am writing to express my concern regarding proposed changes to net neutrality regulations. As a beekeeper operating in Alabama, I depend entirely upon internet access for veterinary consultation and continuing education. Should internet service providers be permitted to charge differential rates based on content type, my operation would be placed at a competitive disadvantage relative to larger enterprises capable of affording premium service tiers. The implications for small agricultural businesses are substantial and warrant regulatory protection of equal access principles.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000264.text: Piano instructor here working for about twenty years streaming recitals for families and selling backing tracks online and my ISP keeps throttling me unless I pay extra which is basically double charging and I can't switch providers because of back problems.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000265.text: Don't get me wrong I'm not some tech expert, but I know what's happening to my business and it stinks. I've got a construction crew and we're constantly uploading videos from job sites—showing problems, getting contractor sign offs, all that stuff. Comcast throttles us bad. Takes forever, connections drop, it's a nightmare and it costs us real money. But honestly that's not even why I'm writing this. I'm scared about my daughter. She's sixteen and homeschooled and she learns everything from YouTube videos. If ISPs start charging more to speed up certain websites, her education becomes something only rich families can afford.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000266.text: Twenty years teaching piano and streaming recitals online for families to watch. Also sell backing tracks. My internet provider throttles uploads unless I upgrade. That's paying twice for the same service. Back issues mean I can't physically switch providers or build multiple systems. This situation is genuinely harming my ability to work.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000267.text: I'm no expert on ISP technology so take this with whatever grain of salt applies, but I know what's happening in practice and it's not good. My construction crew is constantly uploading site videos—approval documentation, contractor communication, that sort of thing—and Comcast keeps slowing us down massively. Double the time, dropped calls, total mess, costs us real money. That part bothers me but it's not the main reason I'm here speaking up. My daughter's sixteen, we homeschool her, and YouTube is basically her entire curriculum—welding, chemistry, history, science, you name it. If ISPs start charging extra just to make certain websites load at normal speeds, what happens to kids whose families can't afford the premium tier? Education shouldn't be a luxury service.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000268.text:
Look, I work with a lot of communities that don't have much money and don't have a lot of power, and what I've noticed over the past few years is that internet access is basically the only tool we have left to actually organize anything at scale. When we're trying to coordinate across different neighborhoods in Oakland, when we're trying to get tenants connected so they can share information about what's happening with their landlords or their rents, we depend on being able to video call and share documents without paying extra for it, and honestly without the ISPs deciding that our organizing work isn't worth the bandwidth.

I've literally watched video calls drop during rent strike coordination sessions and I'm pretty sure it wasn't an accident. If we lose net neutrality and companies can just start charging more for certain types of traffic or slow things down when it doesn't benefit them, then organizing becomes a luxury that only people with money can actually do. And that means poor communities and communities of color just get left behind, which is exactly what's been happening anyway but this would make it official and legal.

The internet was supposed to be this democratizing thing where everyone had a shot at communication and information, and yeah maybe that was naive but at least we had a chance. Without net neutrality protections that chance disappears completely. We need this policy to stay in place or get stronger.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000269.text: This matters because my grandchild depends on internet access for remote university coursework. If broadband companies were allowed to slow down educational platforms or charge extra, they'd be creating artificial obstacles to learning. That's not okay. Net neutrality protects educational equity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000270.text: Eight hundred acres near Decorah won't be profitable without reliable internet access to farming technology. Net neutrality ensures my ISP can't create a two-tiered system where wealthy corporations get fast access and smaller operations get throttled. This rule keeps agriculture fair across all farm sizes.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000271.text: Precision agriculture is necessary now, not optional, for 800 acres. I depend on multiple software platforms requiring reliable bandwidth. Without net neutrality, ISPs could charge ag-tech companies extra fees, effectively creating slow lanes for farmers who won't or can't pay. That's unfair to independent operators.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000272.text:
Look, I spent forty-two years in the union doing electrical work, and I know what happens when big money gets to squeeze out the little guy. That's exactly what's going on right now with these internet companies and they're doing it in a way most people don't even realize. My grandson, he's trying to run a coaching business for kids who want to get into esports, streaming lessons and tips on Twitch and YouTube, but he can't compete because these big streamers are paying the telecom companies to speed up their stuff while my kid's stream keeps buffering. It's the same game they pulled on small electrical contractors back in the eighties when the big chains started muscling in. You either paid their way or you were out of business.

Nobody talks about this but the phone and cable companies are literally becoming a digital cartel if we don't stop them right now. They control the pipes so they control who wins and who loses online, and that's not how America's supposed to work. I watch a lot of videos about what's really happening with the towers and the monopolies and it's all connected—these companies want total control and net neutrality is the only thing standing between us and that. Without it every small business, every startup, every regular person trying to make it is gonna get run over by whoever has the deepest pockets to pay for the fast lane.

The commission needs to keep net neutrality. Period. Don't let them carve up the internet for the rich.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000273.text:
Look, I run a truck stop here in Breezewood and I've been doing this for twenty-three years now. We're right off the interstate so we get drivers coming through all hours—guys who are stuck here for six, eight, ten hours between runs, and you know what they do? They sit in my WiFi area and call their wives, their kids, their mothers back in India or Pakistan or wherever home is for them. That WiFi is not some luxury thing for me, it's part of why they stop here instead of down the road. It's why they trust me.

Now what I'm hearing about is that the big internet companies want to charge more money for faster speeds, and that means the video calls these guys depend on could get slow, or cost me more to provide, or get throttled down to nothing. I don't have the money to pay extra fees so my customers can actually see their families' faces when they call. And these are working people—they're already stretched thin, paying for fuel, missing home. If the internet becomes a two-tier system where rich companies get fast lanes and everybody else gets left behind, my business suffers but worse, my customers suffer.

I've built something here based on treating people right, giving them a decent place to rest, a clean bathroom, good coffee, and yes, a connection to home. If net neutrality goes away, that all gets harder. The FCC needs to keep the internet fair for everybody—not just the companies that can afford to pay extra. Please protect net neutrality.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000274.text:
I spend my days teaching people in their seventies and eighties how to navigate the internet—how to refill prescriptions, check their bank balances, video call their grandchildren. Most of them come to the library because home internet is either too expensive or too unreliable where they live. These aren't people with disposable income to absorb ISP surcharges.

Last month, one of my students, Dorothy, finally got comfortable enough with the computer to do her own telehealth appointments instead of taking three buses to see her cardiologist. She was so proud. So was I. But if the FCC allows throttling, if health services end up in some "premium" tier, what happens to Dorothy? What happens to the dozens of others who've only just found a way to manage their lives with dignity?

I'm not naive about how internet companies operate. I collect old computer manuals—the detritus of technologies we thought would last forever—and watching how quickly things become obsolete teaches you something about whose interests get prioritized in tech. It's never the person with the least power.

Net neutrality isn't some abstract principle. It's whether my students can access the same internet I access. It's whether a senior citizen in rural Vermont can afford to manage her own health. It's about not creating a system where necessity itself becomes a luxury good.

Keep the internet open. Not for ideological reasons. For Dorothy.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000275.text:
I've been teaching digital literacy at the Burlington Public Library for nearly a decade, mostly working with older adults on fixed incomes. What strikes me most forcefully about net neutrality is how it affects people who are already at a disadvantage. My students aren't abstract policy arguments—they're real people trying to access telemedicine, apply for jobs online, or help grandchildren with schoolwork.

Last month, one of my regulars, Margaret, came in distressed because her granddaughter's school had moved to Zoom for hybrid learning. Her internet package wouldn't support the video without buffering constantly. The idea that someone might have to pay extra just to participate in their grandchild's education seems fundamentally wrong to me. These aren't luxury services we're discussing. They're becoming necessities.

Here's what troubles me most: the argument that paid prioritization drives innovation assumes everyone operates from the same starting line. They don't. When broadband becomes tiered by content type or source, we're essentially creating digital literacy classes where only some people can afford admission. It's the opposite of what public libraries have always stood for.

I've spent my career believing that access to information is a right, not a privilege. Net neutrality protects that principle in the modern world. Without it, we're telling millions of people—the ones who need opportunity most—that the internet isn't quite for them.

Please keep the internet open.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000276.text: You know what I've learned in 20 years of analyzing how markets break? It's that when information gets distributed unfairly, everything falls apart. Net neutrality rollback does exactly that—creates a pay-to-play internet where ISPs decide who wins. That's not regulation, that's market failure by design. We're basically rebuilding the 2008 conditions. And yeah, personal stake here: my daughter runs a music platform. How's she supposed to compete if she can't afford paid prioritization? This is wrong structurally AND morally.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000277.text: After analyzing financial markets for two decades, I recognize the signs of structural market failure in this proposal and frankly I'm alarmed. Paid prioritization creates an information hierarchy—some companies get better access, some don't—and that's exactly the dynamic that preceded 2008. The internet shouldn't be a two-tiered system where ISPs extract rent from innovation. My daughter's trying to build a streaming platform and this would essentially kill her before she starts competing with entrenched players. It's bad policy and worse economics.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000278.text: Don't get me wrong I'm not some tech expert, but I know what's happening to my business and it stinks. I've got a construction crew and we're constantly uploading videos from job sites—showing problems, getting contractor sign offs, all that stuff. Comcast throttles us bad. Takes forever, connections drop, it's a nightmare and it costs us real money. But honestly that's not even why I'm writing this. I'm scared about my daughter. She's sixteen and homeschooled and she learns everything from YouTube videos. If ISPs start charging more to speed up certain websites, her education becomes something only rich families can afford.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000279.text: Writing as a small-scale beekeeper in Alabama, I must express serious concern about proposed net neutrality modifications. My business operations are entirely dependent upon equitable internet access. Video-based consultation with university agricultural specialists and access to online educational materials are necessary components of my colony management strategy. Permitting internet service providers to implement premium pricing or content prioritization would create unjust competitive conditions systematically favoring larger enterprises. Such regulatory changes would threaten the viability of small agricultural operations.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000280.text:
I work with people in their seventies and eighties who are discovering the internet for the first time, often out of necessity rather than choice. Last month, a woman named Dorothy came to the library in tears because she'd finally been able to video call her grandson in California—her first grandchild, born while she was recovering from a stroke. She'd missed the first three months of his life. Now she uses our public WiFi twice a week for telehealth appointments that keep her out of the hospital.

The thing that terrifies me is how fragile this access actually is. These aren't people with money for premium tiers or multiple devices. They're choosing between medications they need and keeping up their internet bill. If ISPs start prioritizing certain health sites over others, or if telehealth gets throttled because it doesn't line corporate pockets, we're not talking about inconvenience—we're talking about people's lives.

I've spent thirty years helping people navigate information, and I've never seen a tool that matters quite like this one does. Yes, there's a lot of technical jargon around net neutrality, but the principle is simple: access shouldn't depend on who can afford to pay more. The internet isn't a luxury good in 2024. It's how my students access their doctors, their bank accounts, their families.

Protect net neutrality. My students deserve the same internet as anyone else.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000281.text: The technical details of internet service provision admittedly escape me, however I feel compelled to address a pressing concern affecting both my livelihood and my family. As a small business owner in construction, video uploads have become essential to our operations. Comcast's service throttling has substantially degraded performance. More concerning, however, is the potential impact on my homeschooled daughter's access to educational content. Should broadband providers begin implementing differential pricing schemes, educational equity for lower-income families would be severely compromised.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000282.text: You might not realize this, but some people like me actually earn their living streaming competitive games. So when the ISP started degrading my traffic after I said no to their premium package—ping jumped from 12ms to 45ms—that directly impacted my income. I shouldn't have to spend $12,000+ on workarounds, but I did, because fair internet access isn't guaranteed. Net neutrality rules protect people whose careers depend on consistent connectivity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000283.text:
Look, I'm going to be straight with you. I organize tenant rights work in Oakland, and a huge part of what I do now lives on the internet. We use video calls to reach people who can't make it to meetings—single parents working multiple jobs, elderly folks on fixed incomes. When ISPs throttle our calls or make video buffers like crazy, we literally can't do our work. I've watched organizing sessions fall apart because the connection got so bad people gave up.

And it's not just that. I run a community aquaponics network—we share farming data with neighborhoods dealing with food insecurity. The whole thing depends on reliable, affordable broadband that doesn't get held hostage by providers deciding which data gets priority. If Comcast or whoever decides agricultural education isn't profitable enough for decent speeds, we lose access to the tools that let us help people grow their own food.

I also run a YouTube channel about speed stacking—competitive cup stacking, it's a real sport—and I've got like 12,000 subscribers now. Small audience, I know, but that community matters to people. I've seen enough to know that when ISPs can create fast lanes, smaller creators and organizing groups and community projects get left behind. That's just how it works.

Net neutrality isn't some abstract thing. It's whether regular people can actually use the internet to do the work we believe in, or whether it becomes another system that only works if you've got money to pay for priority access. Keep the internet open.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000284.text:
The efficient market hypothesis presupposes that all market participants have access to material information at roughly the same time; when that condition breaks down, you get precisely the kind of information asymmetries that preceded 2008. I'm watching the same dynamic potentially unfold in broadband, and it troubles me deeply.

My daughter runs a music streaming application—nothing massive, but genuinely innovative work she's done with some classmates—and it operates on margins thin enough that any ISP-imposed throttling or paid prioritization essentially creates a pay-to-compete barrier against entrenched players. That's not competition; that's extraction rent. If Spotify can pay for fast lanes and my daughter cannot, the market has ceased to function as a discovery mechanism for actual quality and has become instead a distribution mechanism for whoever can afford the toll.

But here's what really keeps me up at night: we've seen this movie before. When information flows asymmetrically, when some participants in a market have privileged access or speed, you don't get efficient outcomes—you get fragility. You get hidden correlations between system participants that nobody fully understands until they suddenly matter enormously. The financial crisis wasn't primarily about greed; it was about opacity masquerading as liquidity.

Without robust net neutrality protections, ISPs become gatekeepers with privileged information about traffic patterns, emerging applications, and competitive dynamics. That's not a network; that's a toll road operated by someone who's simultaneously a competitor.

I'm asking the Commission to preserve open access rules. Not for ideological reasons, but because functioning markets require symmetrical information, and functioning democracies require functioning markets.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000285.text: Twenty years teaching piano and this internet situation is really getting to me. Stream recitals online so families can watch from wherever they are. Sell backing tracks online too. My internet provider keeps throttling unless I pay extra which feels like being charged twice. Can't switch providers or set up multiple systems because of back problems I have. This needs to stop.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000286.text: The intricacies of internet service provision aren't something I pretend to comprehend fully, however the observable degradation in service quality affecting my construction business is undeniable and concerning. Video documentation transmission, critical to operational approval processes, experiences substantial latency. While economically detrimental, my primary apprehension involves educational access. My homeschooled daughter, age sixteen, depends exclusively upon online educational resources, particularly video content. Implementation of prioritized access pricing structures would create prohibitive barriers for families of moderate means seeking equitable educational opportunities for their children.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000287.text:
Yo, so I'm Marcus from Chicago and I make beats and medical coding content online. I know that sounds random but stay with me. The reason I'm writing is because net neutrality actually matters for what I do, and I think people don't realize how messed up things get without it.

Right now I'm trying to build my music promotion on social media. I upload my tracks, I post clips, I try to grow my audience. But here's the thing—if my ISP decides they want to throttle my uploads because I'm not paying extra, while some major label's content flies through on a fast lane, that's not a fair competition anymore. I literally can't compete. My beats take forever to load. Their stuff is instant. That's not because my music is worse. That's because I don't have the money to pay ISP toll booths.

And then on the medical coding side, I'm learning billing software in my spare time. A lot of that training is online videos and documentation. If internet speeds get divided up by who pays what, learning stuff becomes a luxury only rich people can afford. That kills opportunity.

This isn't about free stuff. It's about keeping the internet as a level playing field where talent and hard work actually matter more than how much money you got to throw at your ISP. Net neutrality protects that. Take it away and you're basically saying the internet belongs to whoever's already rich enough to buy priority access.

Keep net neutrality. Please.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000288.text: Net neutrality. Yeah, I'm gonna say something. My kid's taking classes online and if the ISPs start squeezing educational websites or making universities pay extra tolls just to get normal speeds, that's messed up. This is about keeping the internet open for everyone, not letting Comcast decide who gets to learn what.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000289.text:
I grew up on a ranch in Montana with basically no internet until high school, so I know what it's like when you can't access the same stuff everyone else can. Now I'm studying marine biology at UCSB and doing research on kelp forest disease, which means I'm constantly downloading these huge satellite imagery datasets. My lab's internet connection is already pretty maxed out just trying to handle it.

Here's the thing—if ISPs can start throttling research traffic or demanding more money from universities to access data at normal speeds, that puts schools like mine in direct competition with places that have massive endowments. We're literally trying to understand climate change impacts on our local ecosystems, but if those datasets cost extra to access fast, only the rich institutions get to do that work efficiently. That's not how science should work.

I'm not against ISPs making money. I get that they built the infrastructure. But net neutrality isn't about asking for free stuff—it's about making sure the internet stays a level playing field where innovation and research aren't gatekept by whoever's willing to pay extra. A startup shouldn't have to negotiate with Comcast to exist. A public university shouldn't need a donor's check to download climate data.

I'm only 21 and I've already seen how much the internet matters for opportunity. Keeping it open is worth protecting.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000290.text: This is about fairness, plain and simple. My granddaughter does engineering coursework online from Sacramento, and if ISPs could slow down or charge for educational websites, they'd be creating new barriers to learning based on money. Net neutrality prevents that. We need these protections in place.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000291.text:
Look, I'm not gonna pretend I know everything about how the internet works, but I know what's happening to me right now and it's messed up. I work part-time at a gas station and I'm trying to finish my associates degree online through Northeast Mississippi Community College. The problem is our ISP throttles video after I hit 50GB a month, which sounds like a lot until you're trying to watch lecture videos and do group projects that depend on streaming. My classes buffer constantly. It's ridiculous.

If net neutrality goes away, I'm pretty sure the same company will start charging extra for "education tier" access or whatever they want to call it. And honestly? I can't afford that. Community college is already stretching my budget. The whole point of me doing this online program is because it's cheaper and works around my schedule, but if they start charging premium prices just to access educational content, I'm basically screwed.

I don't understand why a company should be allowed to decide what kind of traffic gets priority based on whether they can make more money off it. That's not how it should work. The internet should be the internet. If I'm paying for internet service, I should get internet service, not some throttled version where they pick winners and losers.

Please don't let them kill net neutrality. Some of us actually need this thing to work fairly.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000292.text: Frankly, as a professional competitive FPS streamer, I'm dependent on stable internet performance. My provider got cute and degraded my service when I wouldn't subscribe to premium gaming, tanking my ping from 12 to 45ms. That's not a minor inconvenience—that's lost income. I've already burned through $12K in equipment trying to work around their discrimination since they won't commit to fair access. Net neutrality protections are essential for people in my position.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000293.text: To whom it may concern: As someone who actively manages honeybee colonies in Alabama, I write to advocate for maintaining net neutrality protections. Internet connectivity functions as essential infrastructure for my agricultural operation. Consultation with university specialists via video conferencing and access to technical resources online represent critical components of effective colony management. Permitting service providers to implement paid prioritization schemes would create unjust market conditions favoring larger, better-capitalized competitors. This regulatory change would disproportionately harm small-scale agricultural enterprises.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000294.text:
Look, I'm gonna be straight with you. I'm working at a gas station right now and going to school online through my community college, and the only way I can watch my lectures is through a mobile hotspot because that's all we got out here in Shuqualak. It's already slow as hell during the afternoons when everybody's using it, and I've missed stuff because the video just won't load in time for my classes.

If you let ISPs start charging these course platforms extra money to get faster speeds, that's basically saying poor people can't go to college. I'm talking about me specifically—I can barely afford tuition as it is. If Coursera or whatever has to pay more just to not get throttled, that cost comes down on students like me. Or the school passes it on. Either way, I'm the one who loses.

Net neutrality isn't some complicated thing when you break it down. It just means everyone gets the same internet. The internet I'm paying for should work the same whether I'm watching Netflix or watching a lecture that I need for a grade. 

I don't know all the technical stuff, but I know what's fair. Don't let the big internet companies make this any worse than it already is. We need the internet to actually work for people trying to get ahead, not just people who can afford to pay extra.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000295.text:
So I'm a marine biology student at UCSB and my lab actually depends on net neutrality to do our work. We've got sensor arrays monitoring kelp forest health off the coast here, and we're constantly uploading data to research partners in Chile and Japan in real time. If an ISP decides to throttle scientific data because it's not paying them extra, our whole $80K NSF grant falls apart. That's not theoretical—that's literally what we're dealing with.

I get that ISPs want to make money, but there's a difference between running a business and creating a system where only the richest institutions and companies can access reliable internet speeds. My lab isn't trying to stream Netflix at the same time we're uploading datasets. We just need consistent, unthrottled access like we're supposed to have.

Also, yeah, I spend way too much time on Discord at 2am talking to marine biologists from other schools about kelp restoration and fish populations, and honestly? That community is how I'm learning stuff that isn't even in my textbooks yet. If ISPs started prioritizing some sites over others, they could basically decide which communities and which research networks deserve to exist.

Keeping net neutrality is just... the baseline. It's not asking for anything crazy. It's just saying the internet should work the same way for everyone. Please don't let them mess this up.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000296.text: This is Marcus Delaney from Alabama and I'm writing because this net neutrality thing actually matters to me in a real way. I've got bees—forty thousand of them—and keeping them alive requires internet access that costs the same as it costs everyone else. I watch videos, I talk to people at Auburn, and that all matters. If big internet companies start charging extra or creating fast lanes for certain content, smaller operations like mine just lose. Bigger beekeepers with more money will get better internet access than me. That's not how competition should work.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000297.text: This is about fairness, plain and simple. My granddaughter does engineering coursework online from Sacramento, and if ISPs could slow down or charge for educational websites, they'd be creating new barriers to learning based on money. Net neutrality prevents that. We need these protections in place.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000298.text: You might not realize this, but some people like me actually earn their living streaming competitive games. So when the ISP started degrading my traffic after I said no to their premium package—ping jumped from 12ms to 45ms—that directly impacted my income. I shouldn't have to spend $12,000+ on workarounds, but I did, because fair internet access isn't guaranteed. Net neutrality rules protect people whose careers depend on consistent connectivity.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000299.text: Can't let this slide without commenting. My family's history includes being shut out of educational opportunities. Now my granddaughter Yuki can learn engineering online from our kitchen. But that only works if ISPs can't throttle school websites or charge universities premium rates. Net neutrality keeps that door open.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000300.text: Frankly, Title II's common carrier obligations are outdated and create regulatory uncertainty that constrains broadband investment and slows innovation plus network expansion to underserved areas. Markets, not government rules, have driven internet advancement. The FCC should restore the light-touch regulatory framework that worked before 2015.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000301.text: The regulatory uncertainty created by Title II common carrier obligations is directly constraining broadband investment to underserved areas. This is backwards policy. Internet technology has advanced exclusively through competitive pressure between providers, not government regulation. The light-touch regulatory framework that existed for two decades before 2015 produced better outcomes. The Commission should reverse the reclassification.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000302.text: The problem is straightforward: Title II common carrier regulations create regulatory uncertainty that discourages broadband investment, particularly to underserved areas. These rules don't fit modern broadband services at all. The internet has always progressed through market competition, not regulatory burden. The FCC should reverse the 2015 reclassification.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000302.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000303.text: Title II's common carrier framework represents regulatory overreach that dampens broadband investment precisely where it's needed—underserved communities. The empirical record demonstrates unambiguously that market forces, not regulatory mandates, have propelled internet innovation. The pre-2015 light-touch regulatory regime fostered two decades of technological advancement and infrastructure expansion. This proven model should supersede the 2015 reclassification.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000304.text: Here's what nobody seems to get: Title II common carrier rules are strangling broadband investment at the exact moment we need to expand networks to communities that still don't have decent service. These regulations were made for telephone monopolies, not for dynamic internet companies. The innovation engine that built the internet runs on competition and market incentives, not regulatory burden. I'm urging the Commission to reverse course on the 2015 decision.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000304.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000305.text: The 2015 Title II reclassification represents a fundamental misalignment between regulatory framework and market reality. Common carrier obligations, designed for natural monopoly conditions, create unnecessary compliance burdens and investment uncertainty in a competitive broadband ecosystem. Market forces have consistently delivered innovation and expansion superior to regulatory mandate. Restoring the prior regulatory classification would eliminate artificial constraints on capital deployment and facilitate network expansion.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000306.text: Title II regulations fundamentally constrain broadband investment through outdated common carrier obligations that create regulatory uncertainty, chilling innovation and slowing network expansion to underserved areas. Every significant internet advancement has resulted from market competition, not government intervention. The FCC should restore the light-touch approach that allowed the internet to flourish for two decades prior to 2015.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000307.text: Title II's common carrier obligations fundamentally misunderstand how internet services operate in 2024. These regulations create the kind of uncertainty that causes companies to delay or scale back broadband expansion projects, particularly in less profitable underserved regions. The historical record is clear: market-driven innovation, not government intervention, built modern broadband. We should restore the regulatory approach that worked for two decades before the misguided 2015 reclassification.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000308.text: Title II regulations are outdated and counterproductive. Before 2015, the internet was booming with investment and innovation because companies competed freely without common carrier rules hanging over them. Now these regulations create so much uncertainty that companies won't invest, especially in areas that are harder to reach. The free market was working perfectly. We should go back to that regulatory approach.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000309.text: Title II is the wrong approach for broadband. These common carrier rules were made for old monopoly telephone companies, not for competitive internet markets. The internet grew so fast before 2015 specifically because companies could invest and compete without heavy regulation. That's what drives innovation and expansion. Now with Title II, companies are scared to invest, especially in underserved areas. We should go back.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000310.text: The issue with Title II is that it's an outdated regulatory model that fundamentally constrains broadband investment. Common carrier obligations create regulatory uncertainty that discourages companies from expanding networks to underserved communities. The free market has been the actual driver of internet innovation throughout its history. Please restore the light-touch framework and reject the 2015 reclassification.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000311.text: Having worked in network expansion, I can confirm that Title II creates real disincentives for deployment. The market-driven regulatory approach of the pre-2015 era produced remarkable innovation and network growth. Imposing common carrier frameworks on broadband fundamentally contradicts successful historical patterns. Net neutrality turned that upside down by giving the FCC authority over online speech delivery. We don't need bureaucrats deciding what reaches our screens. The marketplace should determine these questions, not federal mandates. The FCC should rescind the 2015 rules.

Net neutrality regulations, though well-intentioned, ultimately give the federal government power to control speech online. We the people should decide what content reaches us, not unelected bureaucrats in Washington. As a free American, I believe the marketplace of ideas should stay unburdened by federal mandates. I strongly oppose Title II classification and urge the FCC to restore internet freedom by repealing the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000986.text: Net neutrality regulations, however well-intentioned, ultimately serve to expand federal government authority over digital speech, contradicting First Amendment principles. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-000995.text: Federal net neutrality rules basically amount to giving Washington veto power over digital speech, which... I mean, that's not what free speech is supposed to look like, right? The whole point of the First Amendment is that regular citizens decide what they want to see and hear, not some agency in the capital. Title II has got to go so we can actually have a free internet.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001002.text: It's honestly pretty straightforward when you think about it - digital spaces are just modern extensions of the public square, right? So First Amendment protections absolutely should apply there. Net neutrality rules might sound nice but they basically empower unelected government officials to regulate what content reaches Americans. That's backwards. We should trust people to make their own choices, not have DC mandate what's available. Those 2015 regulations need repealing.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001003.text: I oppose net neutrality regulations because they empower federal government over digital speech in violation of First Amendment principles. Citizens should control content access through market choices, not unelected bureaucrats through Title II mandates. Internet freedom requires repealing the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001004.text: Frankly, I'm troubled by how casually we've accepted government control over speech delivery mechanisms. Net neutrality regulations, while rhetorically appealing, fundamentally shift power from individuals to Washington. The First Amendment protects our right to access information and express ourselves online. Federal agencies shouldn't regulate which ideas reach which people. The FCC must abandon Title II classification.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001005.text: The way I see it, net neutrality is government overreach hiding behind good intentions. Digital spaces need First Amendment protection, which means freedom FROM regulation, not more of it. Federal mandates can't be the answer to internet freedom—they ARE the threat to it. Please restore freedom by repealing Title II.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001006.text: Ever since these net neutrality rules came in 2015, I've worried they'd give too much power to federal agencies over what we see online. We need internet freedom back, which means Title II has to be eliminated and the market needs to work naturally again.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001007.text: The net neutrality rules represent government intrusion into the digital realm where it doesn't belong. Rather than empowering citizens to decide what online content they consume, these regulations hand authority to federal bureaucrats—which fundamentally contradicts First Amendment principles. A genuine marketplace of ideas requires freedom from government mandates, not Title II controls. The 2015 rules should be rescinded to restore internet liberty.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001008.text: The intersection of digital communication and free speech rights demands protection from governmental overreach, yet net neutrality regulations achieve the inverse by empowering unelected federal officials over online content pathways. The 2015 rules and Title II classification should be rescinded.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001009.text: Net neutrality regulations essentially let government control internet speech, which violates the First Amendment. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001011.text: Having watched tech policy develop over the past decade, I'm concerned that net neutrality regulations represent an overreach of federal authority into digital speech spaces. The First Amendment envisions a marketplace of ideas shaped by citizens, not government decree. Title II classification contradicts this vision. The government should not possess gatekeeping authority over digital information. Market mechanisms, not bureaucratic mandates, should determine content accessibility. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001016.text: The way I see it, net neutrality was supposed to help but really just gave the government a bigger say in what we can access online. That's not freedom. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001018.text: Real talk: net neutrality gives too much power to government bureaucrats who shouldn't be deciding what internet content people see. The whole point of the First Amendment is to protect speech FROM government interference, not enable it through back-door regulations. Let markets work. Let people choose. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001019.text: What troubles me is net neutrality lets Washington control our online information access. That goes against everything the First Amendment represents. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001020.text: Federal regulation through Title II is basically saying the government gets to decide how information moves online and that's the opposite of free speech if you ask me. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001041.text: Thing is, net neutrality regulations sound protective but they're really just Washington inserting itself into how speech flows on the internet, which feels unconstitutional when you think about it. The First Amendment should definitely cover digital spaces and that means people—not government—should decide what content we access. Markets handle this way better than federal mandates ever could. Title II classification is overreach. The FCC should scrap those 2015 rules and let internet freedom actually mean something.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001042.text: Here's what I don't get about these net neutrality regs - they're supposed to help but they basically hand the internet to Washington politicians. First Amendment protections should obviously extend online, but instead we got bureaucrats making decisions about what content Americans see. That's backwards. The marketplace of ideas needs freedom, not government mandates. Those 2015 Title II rules gotta go.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001043.text: Net neutrality regulations, well-intentioned as they might be, represent an unacceptable expansion of federal control over digital speech.
