SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001044.text: The premise that federal regulation protects digital free speech fundamentally errs: such mandates transfer authority from distributed market actors to centralized governmental bodies, thereby constraining citizen autonomy. I respectfully urge repeal of Title II and the 2015 framework.

I truly believe Americans deserve the right to choose what we consume, and that means repealing these 2015 Title II rules immediately.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001214.text: Net neutrality regulations represent an expansion of federal power that directly conflicts with First Amendment principles. By imposing Title II classification, the FCC essentially controls what content Americans can access online. This isn't what a free society looks like. We should trust markets and individuals, not bureaucrats. The 2015 rules must go.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001214.org: Phoenix Center for Advanced Legal Studies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001215.text: The First Amendment deserves extension to digital spaces. Net neutrality rules, while well-intentioned, ultimately empower the federal government to regulate what we see online. We citizens should decide what content reaches us, not unelected officials in Washington. As a free American, I believe the marketplace of ideas should stay unburdened by federal mandates. I strongly oppose Title II classification and urge the FCC to restore internet freedom by repealing the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001216.text: The First Amendment should extend to digital realms. Net neutrality regulations, while well-intentioned, ultimately give federal government power to control online speech. We the people should determine what content we access, not unelected bureaucrats in Washington. As a free American, I believe the marketplace of ideas must remain unburdened by federal rules. I strongly oppose Title II classification and urge the FCC to restore internet freedom by repealing the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001217.text: You've probably heard a lot about net neutrality, so I'll keep this short. The rules give government too much power over digital speech. Before 2015, the internet worked fine. Now bureaucrats are involved. That's worse, not better. Americans should control what we see online, not the FCC. Eliminate Title II regulation.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001218.text: The First Amendment deserves digital application. Net neutrality regulations, while well-intentioned, ultimately give the federal government power to regulate what we see online. We the people should determine what content we see, not unelected officials in Washington. As a free American, I believe the marketplace of ideas should stay unburdened by federal rules. I strongly oppose Title II classification and urge the FCC to restore internet freedom by repealing the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001219.text: I've been thinking about this a lot, and the more I consider net neutrality regulations, the more concerned I get about government control of online speech. Title II essentially makes the FCC an internet speech arbiter, which seems really problematic from a First Amendment perspective. We'd be better off trusting people and markets than federal agencies. Eliminate the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001220.text: Net neutrality regulations fundamentally misunderstand what internet freedom requires. By invoking Title II classification, the FCC essentially asserts governmental control over digital communication—antithetical to First Amendment principles. Rather than trusting unelected officials to manage content distribution, we should rely on competitive markets and consumer sovereignty. The 2015 rules represent regulatory overreach that must be immediately reversed to restore genuine online freedom.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001221.text: The First Amendment should extend into the digital realm. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001222.text: Our founding fathers didn't write the Constitution so that unelected officials could tell Americans what we can and cannot access online. The free market has always been better at determining content than government mandate ever could be. I'm asking the FCC to please reconsider Title II and remove these restrictive 2015 regulations.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001366.text: It strikes me as fundamentally un-American to place digital speech platforms under direct government control. Net neutrality accomplished exactly that through Title II classification. Unelected officials now exercise unprecedented power over online information distribution. This violates the spirit of the First Amendment in the digital age. The FCC should immediately rescind these regulations.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001367.text: My concern is straightforward: net neutrality regulations transfer control of online speech from individuals to federal agencies. That's the opposite of what the First Amendment stands for. Unelected officials shouldn't be determining content availability through Title II mandates. The market mechanism was working fine before 2015. Let's restore internet freedom by eliminating these rules and letting competition drive innovation and access.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001367.org: Phoenix Center for Advanced Legal Studies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001368.text: Growing up, we believed the internet was supposed to be free from government control. Net neutrality turned that upside down by giving the FCC authority over online speech delivery. We don't need bureaucrats deciding what reaches our screens. The marketplace should determine these questions, not federal mandates. The FCC should rescind the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001369.text: The First Amendment must apply in the digital realm. Net neutrality regulations, while well-meaning, ultimately hand the federal government authority to regulate online speech. We the people should decide what content reaches us, not unelected officials in Washington. As a free American, I believe the marketplace of ideas should stay unburdened by federal mandates. I strongly oppose Title II classification and urge the FCC to restore internet freedom by repealing the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001370.text: I shouldn't have to explain why government controlling the internet is a bad idea, but apparently I do. Net neutrality regulations did exactly that. They let Washington decide what online speech people can access. That's not freedom. That's government power. I'm opposed to Title II classification and want the FCC to undo this mistake.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001371.text: You've got to understand that net neutrality, despite what proponents say, is fundamentally about government control. Digital spaces are where speech happens now, and they deserve First Amendment protections, not federal regulation. Washington bureaucrats shouldn't decide what reaches us online. I urge you to reverse the 2015 rules immediately.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001371.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001372.text: With all due respect to the regulation's supporters, net neutrality accomplished the opposite of its stated goal. Instead of protecting free speech, it centralized control of digital platforms in government hands. Title II classification placed unelected bureaucrats between citizens and online content. This undermines First Amendment principles. The FCC must repeal these rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001373.text: Net neutrality regulations, despite their proponents' good intentions, effectively transfer content moderation authority from individuals and markets to federal bureaucrats. This fundamentally contradicts principles of free speech and citizen autonomy. Digital expression should flourish through competitive markets, not governmental prescription. Title II classification and the 2015 framework warrant immediate repeal.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001374.text: The First Amendment should protect the digital realm. Net neutrality rules, though well-intentioned, ultimately empower the federal government to regulate online speech. We citizens should decide what content reaches us, not unelected bureaucrats in Washington. As a free American, I believe the marketplace of ideas should remain unburdened by federal mandates. I strongly oppose Title II classification and urge the FCC to restore internet freedom by repealing the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001375.text: As someone who values free speech, I have to say net neutrality regulations worry me. They give too much control to federal agencies that aren't accountable to the people. I believe the internet should remain a free marketplace of ideas, not subject to government mandates. The FCC should repeal Title II classification immediately.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001375.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001376.text: Government regulation of the internet under the guise of 'net neutrality' is fundamentally about speech control. Title II classification gives unelected federal agencies authority they shouldn't have over digital communications. Citizens, not politicians, should decide what content reaches them. The 2015 FCC rules represent an overreach that violates First Amendment principles. I urge immediate repeal.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001376.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001377.text: Fundamentally, net neutrality rules empower unelected officials to control online speech, which goes against constitutional principles. First Amendment freedoms should apply digitally just as they do elsewhere. The marketplace of ideas thrives without government interference. I strongly urge repealing the 2015 net neutrality rules.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001377.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001378.text: It strikes me as fundamentally un-American to place digital speech platforms under direct government control. Net neutrality accomplished exactly that through Title II classification. Unelected officials now exercise unprecedented power over online information distribution. This violates the spirit of the First Amendment in the digital age. The FCC should immediately rescind these regulations.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001379.text: The fundamental issue: net neutrality hands control over digital speech to unelected federal officials. This contradicts basic American principles about free expression. When government classifies internet service as Title II, they're claiming power to regulate what information reaches citizens. That's wrong constitutionally and practically. The marketplace works better than bureaucrats. Those 2015 regulations need repealing.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001380.text: Look, the whole First Amendment thing matters online just like it does anywhere else. When the government starts classifying internet providers under Title II, they're basically saying bureaucrats get to pick winners and losers in what information flows to us. That's not how America's supposed to work. We need the marketplace of ideas to actually function, and that means getting Washington out of the picture. The 2015 rules need to go.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001380.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001381.text: Look, I get that net neutrality was supposed to help people, but let's be real—it just gives Washington more control over what we can say and see. Free speech means free speech everywhere, including the internet. The marketplace of ideas works best when government stays out of it. The FCC should seriously consider repealing those 2015 regulations.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001381.org: National Association of Mutual Insurance Companies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001382.text: I'm deeply concerned that net neutrality regulations amount to disguised government censorship of the internet. Handing Title II authority to federal bureaucrats means Washington controls what Americans see online. That violates the First Amendment's core purpose. People should decide their content, markets should allocate resources, and government should retreat. The FCC must repeal these rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001383.text: Digital speech deserves First Amendment protections, full stop. These net neutrality rules give Washington bureaucrats way too much power over what we see online. The free market works better than government mandates—consumers should choose their content, not have it filtered through some agency. I'm asking the FCC to get rid of the 2015 rules and let the internet stay free.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001384.text: To whom it may concern: Net neutrality regulations represent an overreach of federal authority into digital communications. The First Amendment protections must extend to the internet just as they do other mediums. Government agencies should not possess the power to regulate online content distribution. I respectfully urge the Commission to rescind Title II classification and restore market-based solutions to broadband management.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001385.text: Net neutrality, for all its theoretical benefits, fundamentally undermines First Amendment protections by centralizing speech regulation under federal authority. Citizens should exercise control through market choice, not government mandate. Title II classification exemplifies this regulatory overreach. Repealing the 2015 rules would restore proper balance and internet freedom.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001386.text: The whole point of free speech is that citizens decide what reaches them, not government. Net neutrality regulations flip that on its head by letting Washington control online content distribution. These 2015 Title II rules are fundamentally un-American. The FCC needs to repeal them and actually protect internet freedom.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001386.org: National Association of Mutual Insurance Companies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001387.text: These net neutrality regulations basically let the government control digital speech, which contradicts everything about free speech. First Amendment protections must cover online content too, meaning we need less federal intervention, not more. I'm calling on the FCC to repeal the 2015 rules and restore internet freedom.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001387.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001388.text: Digital speech deserves First Amendment protections, full stop. These net neutrality rules give Washington bureaucrats way too much power over what we see online. The free market works better than government mandates—consumers should choose their content, not have it filtered through some agency. I'm asking the FCC to get rid of the 2015 rules and let the internet stay free.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001389.text: So here's my take: net neutrality might sound like it protects consumers, but what it really does is hand the keys to the kingdom over to the federal government. Title II makes the FCC basically the internet police, deciding what content flows where. That's not how a free country operates. We need to ditch these regulations and let people make their own choices about what they see online.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001389.org: Phoenix Center for Advanced Legal Studies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001390.text: Here's my issue with net neutrality: it puts government in charge of controlling which online content gets to us, and that directly contradicts what the First Amendment is supposed to protect. Washington shouldn't be deciding what we can access on the internet—the free market should handle that. Title II classification is wrong. These rules need to be repealed.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001391.text: Respectfully, I believe net neutrality regulations undermine digital free speech. By giving federal agencies authority over online content distribution, these rules essentially let government control what we see and read. The First Amendment protects our right to access diverse information without government interference. Please eliminate the 2015 Title II rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001392.text: This regulatory approach, while perhaps well-motivated, fundamentally misaligns with First Amendment values by placing content control in governmental hands. Net neutrality regulations essentially grant Washington bureaucrats authority over digital speech pathways. Citizens must retain autonomy regarding online content consumption. Title II reclassification contradicts free market principles and genuine internet freedom. I advocate for rescinding the 2015 regulatory apparatus.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001393.text: Why should Washington bureaucrats control what we see online? Net neutrality regulations do exactly that, violating First Amendment principles. Markets work better than mandates. People should determine what online content matters through competition and choice. The 2015 Title II rules undermine digital freedom and must go.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001393.org: Phoenix Center for Advanced Legal Studies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001394.text: Here's my thing: net neutrality regulations basically let the government decide what we see online, and that's not okay from a First Amendment perspective. We should be choosing our content, not unelected officials. The marketplace of ideas works best without federal mandates choking it. Title II is particularly problematic. The FCC needs to repeal the 2015 rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001395.text: Net neutrality regulations essentially let government control internet speech, which violates the First Amendment. Federal agencies shouldn't decide what content reaches people. Market forces and individual demand should drive online content distribution. Title II classification is problematic. I'm urging the FCC to repeal the 2015 rules.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001395.org: Phoenix Center for Advanced Legal Studies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001396.text: Here's my concern: net neutrality regulations give unelected Washington bureaucrats control over what reaches us online. That's government censorship, basically, even if unintentional. Free people should decide what content matters through markets and choice, not through federal mandates. First Amendment principles demand repealing the 2015 Title II rules.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001396.org: Phoenix Center for Advanced Legal Studies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001397.text: Frankly, I'm tired of pretending net neutrality is anything other than a power grab. These regulations put unelected federal officials between Americans and their information. That's not protection; that's censorship with better PR. The First Amendment means people choose what they consume, markets decide what succeeds, and government stays out of it. Repeal Title II. Now.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001398.text: Think about it: net neutrality puts unelected bureaucrats between Americans and our online content choices. That's not freedom, that's control. First Amendment protections should mean the internet operates freely without federal mandates. Please abandon the 2015 rules and let internet freedom flourish.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001398.org: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001399.text: Net neutrality regulations hand control of online speech to government—exactly what the First Amendment opposes. Federal agencies shouldn't regulate content distribution. Markets and consumer preference should drive what reaches people online. Unelected bureaucrats have too much power under Title II. Repeal the 2015 rules.
SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001399.org: Phoenix Center for Advanced Legal Studies

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001400.text: I gotta say, Title II regulation is just strangling innovation in this industry. Before 2015 everything worked fine and ISPs were investing heavily in networks. Net neutrality is making people think the government should control the internet, which is wrong. Let the market work.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001401.text: Here's my take: net neutrality regulation is bad policy that hurts consumers and innovation. Title II was a mistake in 2015 and remains a mistake today. The free market allocates resources more efficiently than government mandates. Competition and consumer choice drive better outcomes. These rules should be repealed immediately.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001402.text: I oppose net neutrality. Free markets should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom now by repealing the rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001403.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not federal authorities. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom now by repealing the rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001404.text: Federal regulation of the internet via Title II is fundamentally misguided and harmful. The pre-2015 market-based approach worked far better for innovation and consumer welfare. Government mandates stifle progress and discourage investment. Eliminate these counterproductive rules and restore internet freedom now.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001405.text: Every time I hear about net neutrality I think about how well things worked pre-2015 without all these regulations. Title II classification made things worse not better because it discouraged innovation and competition. The free market has always been the best regulator of internet services because companies need to compete for customers. These net neutrality rules are bad policy and should be repealed immediately.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001406.text: The problem with net neutrality is it assumes the government can manage technology better than the private sector. That's backwards. The internet's success came from competition and freedom, not regulation. Title II is just another example of Washington trying to fix something that wasn't broken.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001407.text: The free market is better at this than government. Period. Before 2015 the internet improved because companies competed to offer better service. Title II changed the incentive structure. Now investment is down and bureaucracy is up. Repealing the rules makes sense.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001408.text: Look, I'm not some corporate shill, but the facts are clear: we don't need the federal government controlling the internet through Title II. Competition between ISPs is the actual mechanism that keeps prices reasonable and services improving. The regulations implemented in 2015 have been a disaster for consumers and innovators alike. Remove these rules and let the market function as intended.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001409.text: The Title II classification is fundamentally flawed and needs to be removed immediately. Government overreach through net neutrality rules stifles the very innovation that makes the internet valuable to consumers. Markets functioned efficiently prior to 2015 without heavy-handed federal intervention. Repealing these regulations would unleash private sector competition and actually improve service quality.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001410.text: Repealing net neutrality would unleash innovation that's currently being held back by regulatory burdens. Title II classification was a mistake that treats internet access like a utility instead of a competitive service. Free markets worked before 2015 and they'll work now.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001411.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows technological progress. Restore internet freedom now by repealing the rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001412.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom by repealing these rules now.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001413.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom now; repeal the rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001414.text: The core problem with net neutrality is that Title II treatment assumes government knows better than the market. It doesn't. Before 2015, competitive forces drove improvement without federal interference. Now these rules are preventing investment in better networks and new services. Repeal net neutrality and trust the free market.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001415.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and regulatory action slows progress. Restore internet freedom now by repealing the rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001416.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom now by repealing these rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001417.text: These regulations are counterproductive and we should end them. The internet developed and thrived for years without Title II. Now with heavy-handed government rules we see reduced infrastructure investment and slower innovation. The free market is the better regulator.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001418.text: Federal Title II classification of broadband represents a fundamental mistake in internet policy. Prior to 2015, the market was handling everything efficiently: providers competed, consumers benefited from innovation and choice, and nobody was clamoring for government intervention. Now we've got regulatory overreach killing progress and raising costs. The solution is obvious—restore market competition by eliminating net neutrality rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001419.text: Net neutrality sounds protective but Title II regulation is actually anti-consumer and anti-innovation. Before 2015 the internet industry was thriving with competition and progress. Now we've got government bureaucrats deciding how ISPs can operate, which doesn't make sense. Markets work better than mandates. The solution is to repeal these rules and trust in competition and consumer choice.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001420.text: The core problem with net neutrality is that it replaces market competition with government regulation, which is always worse for consumers and innovation. The internet worked great before Title II. Let's go back to what actually works.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001421.text: My position is simple: government shouldn't be running the internet. Title II regulation creates obstacles to investment and innovation without providing meaningful consumer benefits. The free market worked fine before 2015 and would work better if these rules were repealed. Competition, not regulation, serves consumers best.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001422.text: I oppose net neutrality. Free markets should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom now by repealing the rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001423.text: I'm skeptical of net neutrality because it gives too much power to government agencies instead of letting market forces work. The internet was developing great before these regulations. Title II classification is bad policy that needs to be reversed.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001424.text: My background is in business and it's obvious to me that Title II is counterproductive. Regulation makes companies less willing to invest and innovate because they're too busy complying with bureaucratic requirements. The internet thrived on competition and freedom. We should return to that model.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001425.text: Net neutrality advocates mean well I suppose, but Title II classification was a huge mistake that we should reverse immediately. Before the FCC imposed these rules in 2015, the internet ecosystem was competitive and innovative and working great for everyone. Now companies are hesitant to invest because of regulatory uncertainty. The free market got us here, and the free market should keep leading.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001426.text: Net neutrality rules fail because they replace market mechanisms with regulatory mandates. The internet industry demonstrated that competition drives innovation, service quality, and consumer value. Title II's restrictions contradict this successful model. Repealing these rules restores the conditions for continued progress.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001427.text: Net neutrality rules sound good in theory but the reality is Title II classification is destroying incentives for infrastructure investment. I work with broadband companies and can tell you this firsthand. Before these regulations we were building faster, farther, cheaper. Now? Bureaucracy.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001428.text: I've been observing this whole net neutrality debate for years now, and I remain convinced that government regulation is the wrong approach. The internet flourished under market competition before Title II was implemented in 2015. Since then, investment has dropped off and innovation has slowed noticeably. Consumers are better served by competition than by federal mandates. Repeal these rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001429.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. We must restore internet freedom now by repealing the rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001430.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom by repealing these rules now.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001431.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Restore internet freedom now by repealing these rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001432.text: Honestly I don't understand why the government thinks it needs to control the internet when the free market was doing just fine managing it. Title II happened in 2015 and what did it accomplish? Not much except slowing things down and creating problems. If we want the internet to keep improving we need less government, not more.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001433.text: Having worked in tech for twenty years, I can tell you with certainty that heavy-handed regulation kills innovation faster than anything else. The internet thrived in a competitive marketplace before Title II came along in 2015. Now we're seeing slower deployment of new services and higher prices. Consumers deserve better. Market competition, not federal mandates, is the real solution here.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001434.text: Title II classification was a mistake from the start. The internet didn't have massive problems requiring government intervention. Companies were competing, service was improving, infrastructure was being built. These rules slowed everything down. Repealing them would help consumers and innovation.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001435.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Repeal the rules now to restore internet freedom.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001436.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. Repeal the rules now and restore internet freedom.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001437.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. The internet was fine before 2015 and government regulation slows progress. We need to restore internet freedom by repealing the rules now.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001438.text: Federal Title II classification of broadband represents a fundamental mistake in internet policy. Prior to 2015, the market was handling everything efficiently: providers competed, consumers benefited from innovation and choice, and nobody was clamoring for government intervention. Now we've got regulatory overreach killing progress and raising costs. The solution is obvious—restore market competition by eliminating net neutrality rules.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001439.text: I oppose net neutrality. The free market should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for innovation. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001440.text: Honestly I'm just tired of government pretending it can manage the internet better than the people actually running it. Title II has been a disaster for innovation and investment. Things were better before, will be better after we get rid of these rules. Let the free market do what it does best.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001441.text: My position is simple: government shouldn't be running the internet. Title II regulation creates obstacles to investment and innovation without providing meaningful consumer benefits. The free market worked fine before 2015 and would work better if these rules were repealed. Competition, not regulation, serves consumers best.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001443.text: Honestly kind of concerned about how Title II is hurting things? The internet was developing really well before all this regulation started in 2015, and I worry that government control is just slowing down progress instead of helping consumers. Maybe we should trust businesses to compete on their own and see what happens if we scale back the rules?

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001444.text: I oppose net neutrality. Market competition should regulate the internet, not the federal government. Title II is bad for consumers and bad for technological innovation. The internet was working before 2015 and government regulation slows development. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001445.text: Why do we need Washington bureaucrats deciding how the internet works? SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001446.text: Federal regulation of the internet through Title II is counterproductive and unnecessary. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001447.text: Net neutrality rules are bad for everyone. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001448.text: Net neutrality supporters will tell you government needs to control the internet, but that's backwards thinking. Title II has been a disaster since 2015—blocking innovation, raising costs, slowing down progress. The free market worked for decades without federal intervention. Let's repeal these rules and get back to what actually works: competition.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001449.text: Not to be that guy but government regulation of internet infrastructure is just not the right approach. Markets work better. Before 2015 proof of concept existed. Competition incentivized good service. Title II disrupted that. We should go back to what was working.

SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001450.text: I oppose net neutrality. Market competition should regulate the internet, not federal authorities. SYNTHETIC-NET-NEUTRALITY-2026-001451.text: The internet industry doesn't need Title II regulation. Innovation and competition drive progress, not government mandates. Internet & Television Association.why: Cable industry's primary lobbying arm. Spent $13.6M on FCC lobbying in 2017. Filed comments on every net neutrality docket since 2007.

American Bankers Association.why: Largest US banking trade group. Active on CFPB and FCC rulemakings.

Free State Foundation.why: Telecom-funded think tank publishing pro-deregulation white papers. Frequently cited in industry comments.

TechFreedom.why: Free-market tech advocacy group that consistently filed against Title II.

American Petroleum Institute.why: Oil & gas industry's primary lobbying organization. $7.4M in federal lobbying in 2017.

Americans for Tax Reform.why: Grover Norquist's tax-cut advocacy organization. Heavily involved in coordinated comment campaigns.

Heritage Foundation.why: Major conservative think tank. Comments often coordinate with regulated industries.

Fluent Inc.why: Identified by NY AG as the firm that submitted 4.8M fake net neutrality comments using stolen identities. Settled for $1.7M in 2022.

Opt-Intelligence.why: One of six lead-gen firms named in NY AG net neutrality astroturfing case.

CQ Roll Call (lead-gen).why: Owns 'Voter Voice' platform used to generate bulk comments on behalf of lobby clients.

Voter Voice (Capitol Advantage).why: Mass-comment generation platform. Used by hundreds of trade associations.

American Coalition for Clean Coal Electr.why: Coal industry advocacy group. Active in EPA rulemakings.

Edison Electric Institute.why: Investor-owned electric utility lobby. $9.2M in federal lobbying.

USTelecom Association.why: Wireline telecom industry. $4.8M in lobbying. Filed against Title II in 2014, 2015, 2017.

CTIA - The Wireless Association.why: Wireless carriers' lobby. AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile members. $9.5M lobbying.
